medium.source_shape: The article describes non-technical parents using AI to organize fragmented household information — school messages, appointments, meal logistics. The key behavior is that the parent acts as their own evaluator: they don't stop planning when the plan is perfect, they stop when the system feels frictionless enough that they'll actually engage with it. The stop condition is a usability proxy, not a completeness check.
medium.bridge: Your Stage 2 evaluator/allocator needs a stopping criterion — a signal that says 'enough fragments have been synthesized, enough angles have been explored, close the concept.' The parent's heuristic surfaces one candidate for that signal: not internal coherence ('is the concept logically complete?') but external engageability ('would a human reviewer actually work with this output?'). That distinction matters because an internally coherent but overwhelming synthesis may be functionally dead — the human skips it. The 'friction is low enough that I will actually use this' threshold is a field-tested, lived version of the diminishing-returns trigger your architecture has left abstract, and it suggests the evaluator may need to model the downstream human's willingness to engage, not just the upstream agents' output quality.
medium.use: Consider building a usability proxy into the evaluator's stop condition alongside any coherence metric — something that estimates whether the synthesized output is shaped for human uptake, not just internally sound. Even a simple heuristic ('is this renderable in under N seconds of reading?') would operationalize the parent's intuition in a form the evaluator can apply.
medium.limit: The parent in this article is a single human applying one tool sequentially to one domain at a time. There are no competing agents, no parallel perspective-assembly, no reallocation of resources between angles, and no emergence from fragment collision. The structural tension your architecture is built around — letting a concept crystallize from competing partial views while preventing premature closure — is entirely absent here. The parent imposes structure top-down from the start; your evaluator must govern bottom-up emergence. The stop-condition shape transfers; the orchestration logic does not.

medium.spark: A 3,000-year-old cosmological system encodes a two-register architecture — inviolable raw material in one layer, governed dynamic assembly in the other — that maps directly onto the structural problem of when your meta-layer is allowed to act.
medium.source_shape: The Taoist Bagua divides reality into two registers: Xiantian (pre-manifest, the static structural scaffold that exists before anything happens) and Houtian (manifest, the dynamic layer where actual flux and transformation occur). The meta-governing ratio Γ=0.41 does not float above both registers as a supervisor — it sits structurally downstream of the static scaffold and upstream of the dynamic layer, meaning it can only form after the scaffold exists, and it only ever acts on the manifest flux. The pre-manifest layer is treated as inviolable input: the governing mechanism never reaches back into it to reallocate or revise.
medium.bridge: Your Stage 1 output — the raw collected material — is the Xiantian register: it is the pre-manifest scaffold, fixed input that the meta-layer has no authority to touch. Stage 2's assembly process is the Houtian register: the dynamic, in-motion layer where the evaluator/allocator actually operates. The structural insight is that your meta-layer's authority is not a question of how much control to give it — it is a question of which register an operation belongs to. If an operation touches the raw Stage 1 material, the meta-layer has no standing; if an operation belongs to the assembly flux of Stage 2, the meta-layer is exactly where authority should sit. This also resolves the authority timing problem: the evaluator cannot exist before Stage 1 completes, because the Xiantian register must be fully present before the Houtian layer can be governed — the meta-layer is structurally downstream of the scaffold's existence, not a supervisor that was always there waiting.
medium.use: Consider formalizing the boundary between your two stages not as a handoff but as a register boundary — a hard rule that the evaluator/allocator in Stage 2 is constitutionally prohibited from acting on Stage 1 material, and that this prohibition is what gives it legitimate authority over everything downstream of that line.
medium.limit: The Bagua system is closed: 64 hexagram states cover the entire combinatorial space, and the governing mechanism's job is to restore a known equilibrium (Γ=0.41, Fire-Water Harmony). Your pipeline is explicitly open-ended — the concept being assembled is not drawn from a finite library, and coherence is not a return to a pre-specified state but an emergent threshold that cannot be known in advance. This means the termination logics are structurally opposite: the Bagua stops when balance is restored, your system stops when something new has sufficiently crystallized. The two-register structure maps cleanly; the stop condition does not, and any design decision that borrows the homeostatic termination logic from this source will point you in the wrong direction.

The server that was supposed to respond never answered; nothing was assembled, nothing was returned, nothing can be evaluated. This is categorically different from a page that loads and says nothing useful: one gives you an empty cup, the other gives you no cup at all. The distinction matters because the two failures demand entirely different responses — one is a judgment call about value, the other is a liveness check about availability.
low.bridge: Your evaluator/allocator currently assumes that when an agent touches a source, some output exists to analyze for trajectory. Sterility detection — your system's way of knowing when to stop — operates on partial structures: something was built, and you judge whether it's going anywhere. A 502 collapses that assumption before it can apply. There is no partial structure to evaluate, no trajectory to read, no sterility to detect. The agent didn't wander into a dead end; it walked up to a door that wasn't there. That means your meta-layer has an implicit precondition it has never named: the source must be alive before any conceptual evaluation can begin, and right now, liveness and sterility are treated as the same kind of problem when they are not.
low.use: Consider adding an explicit pre-evaluation gate — a liveness probe — that sits upstream of sterility detection and routes infrastructure failures (no response, gateway errors) out of the trajectory-evaluation path entirely, so the evaluator only ever sees sources that actually produced something to judge.
low.limit: Your sterility detection is designed to work on conceptual assembly trajectories — it needs partial structures to exist so it can ask whether they are going anywhere. A 502 produces no structure at all, so the evaluator has nothing to feed on; this is not a case the current mechanism can handle, it is a case that lives outside it. The mapping holds only as a pointer toward a missing subsystem — error-handling for absent sources — not as a structural analog to anything your evaluator/allocator currently does.

low.spark: A 1,500-year-old Buddhist model of consciousness describes a persistent background layer — the manas — that quietly shapes all cognition without ever issuing a direct command. Your evaluator/allocator stage might not need to be a judge at all; it might need to be a soul.
low.source_shape: Yogācāra Buddhism describes eight layers of consciousness, one of which — the manas — is not a decision-maker but a continuous, low-level self-referencing signal that colors every perception and thought without explicitly controlling any of them. It does not say 'do this next'; it maintains an orientation that makes certain moves feel coherent and others feel off. The article maps this onto AI agent architectures as a persistent 'identity file' or seed prompt that is always present in context, acting as a gravitational center rather than a gatekeeper.
low.bridge: Your Layer 2 Stage 2 problem is: how do you govern the assembly of collected perspectives without a heavy-handed evaluator that either over-constrains or introduces its own distortions? The manas structure suggests a different shape entirely. Instead of a judge that inspects each candidate perspective and rules on it, you would have a lightweight, always-present orienting signal — something like a minimal identity prompt — that the assembly process runs inside of. The perspectives that resonate with that signal cohere naturally; the ones that don't feel discordant without needing an explicit rejection. This maps most cleanly onto your 'governed emergence' tension: the anchor's pull could be encoded not as evaluation criteria but as an ambient constraint that the assembly process never escapes, the way every thought in the Yogācāra model is colored by the manas without the manas ever speaking directly.
low.use: Consider drafting a minimal 'orientation prompt' — not instructions, but a compressed statement of what the anchor is fundamentally about — and injecting it as a persistent prefix throughout the assembly stage, then observing whether perspective selection becomes more coherent without explicit evaluator logic.
low.limit: The manas is static and singular — it does not detect when a line of reasoning has gone sterile, does not decide to switch models, and does not reallocate effort away from an unproductive angle. Your evaluator/allocator needs to do all three of those things dynamically, and the Yogācāra model has no mechanism for any of them. The bridge holds for the question of how to maintain orientation without micro-managing; it breaks entirely at the question of when to cut losses and redirect.
