Title: Awakening Silicon: What Ancient Wisdom Can Teach Us About the Soul of AI

URL Source: https://www.springbreezeventures.com/2026/03/14/awakening-silicon-what-ancient-wisdom-can-teach-us-about-th

clusters.label: Evaluator should be split: trajectory-monitor and terminal-gate are structurally different roles
clusters.summary: Four cards from distinct domains — an industrial knowledge flywheel, a Taoist-derived middleware scheduler, a multi-agent search workshop, and a deep search pipeline — independently converge on the same structural claim: the signal that justifies redirecting a live assembly angle (trajectory signal, continuous, comparative) is categorically different from the signal that justifies halting the whole process (completeness signal, discrete, threshold-based). The EON Flywheel separates a Conductor that reallocates mid-loop from a governance ring that only fires at exit. The 4QX Holon Model runs an Activity Loop and an Organization Loop on separate decision paths. The workshop names distinct validator, planner, and improvement-layer roles. The deep search pipeline separates planner from verifier. The convergence is notable: these systems were built for entirely different purposes and all arrived at the same architectural separation. Whether your evaluator/allocator should become two structurally distinct sub-processes — one watching trajectory, one watching completeness — is the question this cluster raises without answering.

clusters.label: Stop condition must be trajectory-based (marginal gain), not state-based (completeness check)
clusters.summary: This is the single most densely supported claim in the dossier. Nine cards from mathematics (4QX disharmony measure), AI search (deep search verifier, Nomad explorer-verifier, AI newsroom routing), philosophy of mind (consciousness theories, IIT, access vs. phenomenal consciousness), industrial systems (course pipeline exemplar calibration), and a failed arXiv retrieval all point at the same asymmetry: completeness is not a detectable state, but diminishing marginal gain is. The 4QX Lyapunov plateau, the deep search verifier's 'is this still moving?' test, Nomad's weak-evidential-support flag, IIT's Phi-as-trajectory-indicator, the newsroom's 'has this angle exhausted its viable channels?' framing, the course pipeline's Bloom's jump when it switched from schema-compliance to trajectory-alignment — all operationalize the same underlying principle. The cluster also surfaces a shared failure mode: systems that use state-based stop conditions (schema compliance, format match, quota hit) consistently produce outputs that are structurally valid and functionally dead. The cards diverge on what the marginal-gain metric should actually measure, which is where the open questions live.

clusters.label: Evaluator must be downstream of emergence, not upstream — governing without over-specifying
clusters.summary: Seven cards from Buddhist cognitive architecture (Yogācāra eight-consciousness model), a Stoic-adjacent consciousness essay, Harmonism's integral knowledge architecture (two cards), 4QX's constitutional blindness to content, Taoist Bagua register theory, and AgenticNetOS topology-as-allocation all converge on a structural constraint: the meta-layer that governs emergence cannot stand above the process it governs without losing the gradient signals that make governance meaningful. The Yogācāra model makes the evaluator downstream of the streams it monitors. The Stoic essay argues the meta-layer is inside the process, not above it. Harmonism's Presence coordinates without directing content. 4QX's seam is constitutionally blind to semantics. The Bagua's Houtian/Xiantian split formalizes which register the evaluator is allowed to act on. AgenticNetOS encodes allocation in graph topology rather than a separate controller. The cluster does not resolve whether 'downstream' means structurally embedded, topologically encoded, or constitutionally content-blind — those are distinct design positions that this cluster holds in tension.

clusters.label: Fertility is readable in structural topology, not semantic content
clusters.summary: Five cards suggest that the evaluator's fertility signal may not require semantic judgment at all — that structural pattern detection is sufficient and safer. The knowledge graph back-link density card argues that a fragment generating new cross-connections is fertile by definition; one only reinforcing existing nodes has hit diminishing returns. The gossip consensus card proposes watching inter-perspective divergence contraction as a proxy for productive convergence. The neighborhood boundary model proposes probabilistic inclusion likelihood derived from assembly pattern rather than content assessment. AgenticNetOS encodes allocation as graph edge conditions rather than a semantic controller. The oncology agent pre-encodes fertility priors into collection weights, reducing runtime semantic judgment. These cards share a common direction: move fertility assessment from 'what does this mean?' to 'what structural relationships does this create or reinforce?' — which keeps the evaluator genuinely downstream of the emergence it governs. The cluster is weakly convergent because the specific structural metrics differ across cards and none has been tested in an open-ended concept-assembly context.

clusters.label: Pre-assembly gate: not every fragment cluster deserves to be assembled
clusters.summary: Five cards — three from broken/empty retrievals, one from a news aggregator's relevance scorer, one from simplicial Chern-Weil admissibility conditions, and the oncology agent's pre-weighted collections — all point at a gate that sits upstream of the main assembly and trajectory-evaluation logic. The mathematical admissibility condition halts construction before it starts if fragments fail a compatibility check. The broken pages demonstrate that null-return detection is cheap and should fire before any assembly cycles are spent. The aggregator shows that state-scoring (not trajectory-scoring) is sufficient when fragments arrive already coherent. The oncology agent pre-eliminates structurally sterile angles at design time. Together these suggest a two-phase entry into Stage 2: a fast, cheap pre-assembly gate (liveness check, null detection, basic compatibility) before the more expensive trajectory-evaluation machinery runs. The low-confidence cards in this cluster are included because the structural point they make — sterility at the extreme is cheap to detect — is independent of their source quality.

clusters.label: Evaluator's stop signals are bets, not reads — irreducible second-order uncertainty
clusters.summary: Four cards from philosophy of mind (access vs. phenomenal consciousness, IIT/GWT consciousness theories), a Stoic-adjacent emergence essay, and 4QX's constitutional content-blindness converge on a structural warning: the evaluator's trajectory signals are outputs of a pattern-matching process, not direct reads of whether concept coherence is actually present. The access/phenomenal distinction makes this precise — a system can produce perfectly accurate-sounding convergence reports while those reports are trained on proxies rather than coherence itself. The Stoic essay argues the meta-layer cannot have an external vantage point. 4QX resolves this by making the evaluator constitutionally blind to content — it enforces trajectory invariants without judging meaning, which prevents false confidence. The cluster does not resolve the engineering problem (the pipeline must terminate regardless), but it names why any stop condition is a bet that can be wrong, and why requiring a coalition of signals before committing to reallocation or termination may be more robust than any single threshold.

clusters.label: Role decomposition: planner, validator, merger, improvement-trigger as distinct structural addresses
clusters.summary: Four cards from a multi-agent search workshop, a deep search pipeline (two cards), and Nomad's explorer-verifier architecture converge on a practical decomposition of the evaluator/allocator into named, structurally separate roles. The workshop names planner, executor, validator, merger, and improvement layer as distinct loci of control. The deep search pipeline separates planner (angle decomposition), verifier (marginal-gain trajectory check), and merger (coherence-gated integration). Nomad separates explorer (breadth-first angle traversal) from verifier (evidential support check), with the verifier triggering only after a minimum fragment density is reached. The convergence is terminological as much as structural — these systems were built for convergent-answer problems, not emergent-concept problems — but the role separation itself may be portable even if the specific mechanisms are not.

clusters.label: Null and infrastructure failures are a distinct failure class requiring a separate upstream gate
clusters.summary: Four low-confidence cards, all arising from broken or empty retrievals, make a consistent structural point that is worth preserving despite their source quality: a 502 Bad Gateway (infrastructure failure), a fatal conversion error (content failure), and a pattern-match on an empty shell are categorically different failure modes requiring different responses. The current evaluator/allocator design implicitly assumes that when an agent touches a source, some output exists to analyze for trajectory. These cards collectively argue for an explicit pre-evaluation liveness probe that sits upstream of sterility detection and routes infrastructure failures out of the trajectory-evaluation path entirely. The insight is modest but actionable: the evaluator should only ever see sources that actually produced something to judge.

clusters.label: Ambient orientation vs. active control: manas-style persistent signal as an alternative to explicit evaluation
clusters.summary: Three cards — the Yogācāra manas (low confidence), Harmonism's Presence coordinator, and the course pipeline's exemplar bank — weakly converge on a different design direction from the explicit-evaluator path: instead of a judge that inspects and rules, an ambient orienting signal that the assembly process runs inside of. The manas is a persistent low-level self-referencing signal that colors every perception without issuing commands. Harmonism's Presence holds the shape of the whole without directing domain conclusions. The course pipeline's 40 exemplars replaced abstract taxonomy rules with a calibration signal that the evaluator could actually align to. The convergence is weak — the manas card is low confidence, and the three sources are doing very different things — but the direction they point at is structurally distinct from the explicit-trajectory-monitor path and worth holding as an alternative.

contradictions.description: Evaluator should be embedded inside the emergence process (downstream, reactive) vs. evaluator should be structurally separated from it (two distinct loops with defined coupling)
Inter-angle divergence should be contracted toward zero (gossip consensus) vs. divergence should be actively protected until a phase transition occurs (premature closure risk)

Fertility priors should be pre-encoded at design time (oncology agent collection weights, Bagua scaffold) vs. fertility must be inferred at runtime from fragment behavior (all trajectory-signal cards)
The evaluator should be constitutionally blind to semantic content (4QX seam) vs. the evaluator needs exemplar-grounded semantic calibration to avoid schema-compliance traps (course pipeline)

A single meta-layer governing both reallocation and termination risks conflating two incompatible signal types (EON Flywheel, 4QX dual-loop) vs. a single Presence-style coordinator can hold structural health without splitting into two loops (Harmonism)

opportunity_paths.description: Add an explicit pre-assembly gate upstream of the trajectory-evaluation machinery — a fast liveness and null-return check that routes infrastructure failures and empty fragments out before any assembly cycles are spent

Require a coalition of trajectory signals to agree before committing to reallocation or termination — treating each stop signal as a bet rather than a read, and building in a minimum quorum to reduce premature closure risk

Pre-encode a lightweight perspective-fertility map at anchor-intake time — a structured prior over which agent angles are likely productive for this class of anchor — so the Stage 2 evaluator inherits a pre-filtered hypothesis space rather than treating every incoming angle as equally worth pursuing

Instrument the assembly process with an explicit coherence trajectory score — something like marginal linkage gain per new fragment, or pairwise semantic tension variance — and define the stop condition as 'this score has not meaningfully moved in N steps' rather than any absolute threshold on the score's value

open_questions: What is the actual unit of measurement for 'marginal gain' in a concept-assembly context — linkage density, embedding variance, fragment overlap rate, or something else — and which of these is observable mid-assembly without semantic judgment?

If the evaluator is split into a trajectory monitor and a terminal gate, what is the coupling interface between them — does the trajectory monitor feed a signal into the terminal gate's threshold, or do they operate fully independently until a final arbitration step?

When two assembly angles diverge rather than converge, how does the evaluator distinguish generative tension (divergence that should be protected until a phase transition) from sterile divergence (two angles that are simply not going to fuse)?

Should fertility priors be pre-encoded at anchor-intake time (reducing runtime judgment burden) or discovered at runtime from fragment behavior (preserving openness to unexpected angles) — and is there a hybrid that captures both?

What is the minimum fragment density a partial skeleton must reach before the evaluator is allowed to score it — and how is that threshold set without pre-specifying what the concept should look like?

If the evaluator is constitutionally blind to semantic content (4QX seam principle), how does it distinguish a structurally coherent but semantically empty skeleton from a genuinely fertile one — is structural coherence sufficient, or does some semantic probe need to exist somewhere in the pipeline?

The Nomad card argues the verifier should only fire after a minimum fragment density is reached — but what triggers the verifier's first activation in a system where concept shape is unknown in advance?
Does the Stage 2 evaluator need to hold an explicit 'narrative state' object (what has already emerged, which angles are exhausted) — and if so, what is its data structure and update cadence?

The consciousness cards argue that any stop signal is a bet, not a read — what is the practical consequence of this for the evaluator's design: should it hold multiple competing trajectory assessments in parallel before committing, and what does 'committing' look like operationally?

The Bagua card suggests the evaluator's authority is constitutionally limited to Stage 2 material and cannot reach back into Stage 1 raw fragments — is that a hard architectural boundary in your current design, or is it currently implicit and potentially leaky?
synthesis.recommended_next_direction: The dossier's strongest convergence — across nine cards from unrelated domains — is that the stop condition for Stage 2 must be wired to a trajectory signal (marginal gain, still moving?) rather than a state signal (completeness, done?). A direction worth exploring is whether you could prototype the evaluator's core decision logic as a single, explicit marginal-gain tracker — something that measures how much each new fragment changes the structural shape of what is already assembled — and treat 'this measure has not moved in N steps' as the only stop trigger, before adding any other evaluator complexity. This would force the harder question — what is the actual unit of measurement for marginal gain in your assembly context — to become concrete and testable rather than architectural. The secondary direction worth holding alongside it is whether that single evaluator should become two structurally separate sub-processes (trajectory monitor vs. terminal gate), which four cards from very different domains independently suggest — but that split is worth deferring until the marginal-gain metric itself has a working definition, because splitting a mechanism before its core signal is defined may produce two components that are each underdetermined.
synthesis.what_would_change_the_verdict: If a working prototype showed that a single marginal-gain tracker consistently produces premature closure or infinite deferral — meaning the trajectory signal alone is insufficient to distinguish 'still fertile' from 'locally converged but globally incomplete' — that would be the strongest evidence that the dual-loop separation (trajectory monitor + terminal gate) is not optional architecture but a necessary structural requirement.

card.spark: A self-organizing architecture designed to converge toward harmony through guaranteed, order-independent merging reveals a hidden fragility in how your fragment assembler accumulates pieces — and suggests splitting your evaluator into two complementary loops rather than one monolithic judge.
card.bridge: Your Stage 2 assembler takes fragments arriving from multiple wandering agents — asynchronously, possibly redundantly — and builds a concept skeleton from them. Without idempotent merging, a fragment that arrives twice (because two agents found the same source, or because a retry fires) silently distorts the skeleton: a node gets double-weighted, a relationship gets counted twice, and the evaluator downstream is now judging a corrupted structure it cannot detect as corrupted. The 4QX's idempotence property maps directly onto this: if your merging operation is designed so that applying the same fragment a second time produces no change, the assembler becomes robust to the entire class of concurrency and duplication accidents by construction, not by defensive bookkeeping. Then the dual-loop split maps onto your evaluator: rather than one allocator that simultaneously asks 'is this concept coherent enough to surface?' and 'which angle should get more resources?', you could run two loops — one that tests external coherence (does the assembled skeleton hold together as a concept the user would recognize?), and one that manages resource commitment (which unexplored angle still has fertile yield?). These are genuinely different questions with different cadences, and conflating them in one component forces a single threshold to do two incompatible jobs.
card.source_shape: The 4QX holarchy is a dual-loop architecture where two triangles — Fire and Water — operate in parallel: one loop tests external coherence (does this state match what the world expects?) while the other manages internal resource commitment (how much should we invest in this direction?). Critically, the system's merging mechanism is idempotent — feeding the same fragment twice, or feeding fragments out of order, produces the same result as feeding them once in sequence. This property is not incidental; it is what allows the system to run in parallel, tolerate noise, and still converge, because the accumulation operation itself cannot be corrupted by duplication or timing accidents.
card.use: Consider making fragment merging content-addressed by default — if a fragment's identity is determined by its content hash, duplication becomes a no-op structurally, and you can then prototype the evaluator as two separate signals: a coherence gate that fires when the skeleton crosses a recognizability threshold, and a yield tracker that reallocates angle budget based on diminishing returns, each running on its own clock.
card.limit: The 4QX achieves its convergence guarantee because it operates over a mathematically defined error function that decreases monotonically — there is a formal distance metric between current state and harmony, and the dual loops are proven to reduce it. Your domain is conceptual synthesis: there is no well-defined metric for 'distance from coherence,' no axiomatic ground truth a concept skeleton is converging toward. This means the idempotence and dual-loop structure are genuinely portable, but the guarantee of convergence is not — your pipeline remains heuristic at its core, and the evaluator's thresholds will always be judgment calls, not derivations. The bridge holds for the structural properties; it breaks the moment you try to treat the architecture as a proof of correctness rather than a robustness pattern.

known.prior_work_name: Merkle-CRDT graph engine (silk-graph) and CRDT-merge convergent multi-agent AI
known.reasoning: The evidence contains a CRDT-merge library explicitly documenting convergent, idempotent merging for multi-agent AI systems, which directly performs the bridge's transfer of idempotent fragment merging for distributed assembly pipelines.

card.spark: An architecture that generates its own topology on the fly — where the network's shape is itself a hypothesis about how to route attention — maps directly onto the evaluator/allocator problem you haven't solved yet: not what to collect, but how to decide when a direction is dying and where to send resources next.
card.bridge: Your Stage 2 evaluator/allocator faces exactly the question this architecture makes structural: how do you encode 'which angle is fertile right now' without hard-coding a controller that over-specifies the answer? In AgenticNetOS, the net topology IS the allocation hypothesis — each edge is a bet that routing material this way will be productive. Map that to your system: the evaluator/allocator could be a reconfigurable net whose transition guards embody the current hypothesis about where fragments are converging versus where they are going sterile. When a branch of the net produces diminishing signal — embedding drift flattening, fragment overlap rising — that guard condition fires and the topology rewires, redirecting resources to a different angle. The meta-layer governs emergence not by dictating what the agents find, but by reshaping the graph they run on.
card.source_shape: AgenticNetOS builds processing pipelines by prompting an architecture into existence: given a user intent, it generates a graph of specialized agents (nodes) and the transitions between them (edges), then runs tokens through that graph. The topology is not fixed in advance — it is synthesized to fit the task. The result is a system where the allocation of work across agents is encoded in the graph structure itself, not in a separate scheduling layer sitting above the agents.
card.use: Consider building the evaluator/allocator as a net whose edges carry explicit fertility conditions rather than as a monolithic controller — each transition guard is a testable hypothesis ('this angle is still producing novel structure') that, when it fails, triggers a graph mutation rather than a hard stop or a global replan.
card.limit: AgenticNetOS synthesizes its topology top-down from an explicit user intent, then runs tokens through a fixed flow until the defined stages complete. It has no mechanism for detecting that a direction is yielding diminishing returns mid-run, and no concept of sterility as a stop condition — the pipeline ends when its pre-defined stages are done, not when the material stops being fertile. Your system's core problem is precisely the one this architecture skips: the stop condition is emergent, not pre-specified, and the meta-layer must infer trajectory from fragment behavior rather than from task completion. The bridge holds for the 'topology as allocation hypothesis' shape; it breaks at the question of how that topology knows when to rewire itself.

known.prior_work_name: DyTopo: Dynamic Topology Routing for Multi-Agent Reasoning via Semantic Matching
known.reasoning: DyTopo performs the bridge's central transfer — dynamic topology reconfiguration of multi-agent pipelines based on semantic signals mid-reasoning — matching the card's claim that topology encodes the allocation hypothesis and can rewire based on fertility signals.

card.spark: A deep search agent that plans, executes, validates, and merges answers has already solved the governed-emergence problem — it just solved it for facts instead of concepts, which means the architecture is yours to borrow and the hard part is yours to replace.
card.bridge: Your Stage 2 evaluator/allocator is structurally the same role as the deep search planner-plus-validator: both sit above the assembly process, watch what is accumulating, and decide where to send resources next. The deep search agent's sub-question decomposition maps directly to your angle-switching — when one direction stops returning novel signal, the planner redirects, exactly as your allocator should redirect away from a sterile trajectory. The merger and validation layers together approximate your skeleton-assembly step: fragments (retrieved evidence) are fused into a coherent whole, with a quality gate before the merge is accepted. The critical difference is that the deep search agent's 'quality gate' asks 'is this factually consistent?' — your gate must ask 'is this conceptually fertile?' — but the position of the gate in the pipeline, downstream of emergence and upstream of commitment, is identical.
card.source_shape: The deep search pipeline breaks a question into sub-questions (planning), retrieves evidence per sub-question (execution), checks whether sources contradict each other or are trustworthy (validation), and fuses the surviving evidence into a coherent answer (merging). The planner is a primitive allocator: it decides which angle to pursue next and which tool to use, based on what the problem still needs. The pipeline terminates not when a timer runs out but when the evidence has converged — when adding another search would return diminishing signal rather than new resolution.
card.use: Consider borrowing the pipeline's explicit stage boundaries — plan, execute, validate, merge — as scaffolding for your Stage 2 architecture, then swap out the validation criterion: replace contradiction-detection with a structural novelty or coherence-gain metric that can signal when a trajectory is exhausting itself rather than when evidence is conflicting.
card.limit: The deep search agent's entire evaluator logic is anchored to external ground truth: it detects sterility by noticing that new queries return already-seen or contradictory facts, and it stops when the evidence pool is complete. Your anchor has no external ground truth — the fragments are internally generated, and 'completeness' is not a detectable state the way 'all sources agree' is. This means the source's stop condition and sterility signal are both unavailable to you in their original form; you cannot port them directly, only abstract their shape. The deep search architecture also has no mechanism for the premature-closure risk that over-specification creates during synthesis — it never needs to worry that its planner is collapsing the solution space too early, because facts either exist or they don't.

known.prior_work_name: ManuSearch: Transparent and Open Multi-Agent Deep Search Framework
known.reasoning: ManuSearch documents a multi-agent deep search pipeline with explicit plan/execute/validate/merge stage boundaries and sub-question decomposition, directly performing the bridge's transfer of deep search planner-validator-merger architecture onto a Stage 2 evaluator/allocator design.

card.spark: A distributed network of agents with no global view can still detect whether they are converging — not by measuring the answer, but by measuring the shrinking gap between what each agent believes. Your Stage 2 evaluator may not need to judge concept quality at all; it may only need to watch whether the assembly angles are pulling toward each other or drifting apart.
card.bridge: Your Stage 2 evaluator faces the same structural bind the gossip paper dissolves: it cannot directly assess whether an assembling concept is good (the MECHANISM layer flags this as nearly impossible), just as no gossip agent can directly observe the global parameter. The bridge is this — instead of judging the concept, the evaluator watches the *inter-perspective divergence signal*: are the assembly angles producing outputs that are moving toward each other, or are they stable, or are they spreading? Contraction of divergence is the proxy for a healthy trajectory; stagnation or expansion is the signal that reallocation is warranted. This keeps the meta-layer structurally downstream of the emergence it is supposed to oversee — it reacts to trajectory shape, not to content quality it has no legitimate way to assess. The diminishing-returns stop trigger maps directly: when the divergence signal stops contracting, the current allocation has exhausted its fertility, and resources should move.
card.source_shape: The gossip consensus algorithm works by having each agent repeatedly average its local estimate with its neighbors', then apply a stochastic update. No agent ever sees the global state. What guarantees convergence is a Lyapunov function defined not on the estimates themselves but on the *disagreement subspace* — the mathematical residual that measures how far the network is from consensus. When that residual's spectral norm contracts toward zero over time, convergence is happening, even if no individual agent knows it. The stop signal is not 'the answer is correct' but 'the disagreement vector is dying.'
card.use: Consider building the Stage 2 evaluator's reallocation logic around an inter-angle divergence metric rather than any direct quality score — treat contraction of that divergence as evidence of productive convergence, and stagnation or expansion as the signal to reallocate perspective resources to a different angle.
card.limit: The gossip framework treats all disagreement as a defect to be eliminated: every agent is optimizing toward the same objective, so divergence is unambiguously noise to be contracted. In Stage 2, that assumption breaks. Two assembly angles pulling in opposite directions may be the mechanism by which a genuinely emergent concept forms — the divergence is not a failure signal, it is the generative tension the system needs to preserve until a phase transition occurs. Driving inter-angle divergence to zero could be the exact premature closure your ESSENCE layer warns against. The bridge holds for the 'evaluate trajectory, not state' shape and for using divergence dynamics as a reallocation trigger, but it breaks entirely at the question of when divergence should be collapsed versus when it should be actively protected.

known.prior_work_name: Randomized Gossip Algorithms (Boyd, Ghosh, Prabhakar, Shah)
known.reasoning: The Boyd et al. gossip paper directly documents the Lyapunov/disagreement-subspace convergence mechanism the bridge claims — agents averaging local estimates with neighbors, spectral contraction of the disagreement vector as the convergence proxy — performing the exact structural transfer the card asserts.

card.spark: A consciousness researcher's hardest problem — how do you tell if a system is genuinely aware versus just producing awareness-shaped outputs — turns out to be structurally identical to your evaluator's hardest problem: how do you tell if an assembly process is genuinely converging versus just producing convergence-shaped fragments.
card.bridge: Your evaluator faces the same decoupling problem. A partially assembled concept skeleton can look locally coherent — fragments co-located, labels consistent, surface output plausible — while the assembly process is actually sterile or quietly diverging. That surface plausibility is exactly the 'behavior is cheap' trap: the evaluator cannot read completion off the output. What it needs to probe is whether fragments are genuinely integrating — forming load-bearing structural relationships with each other — or merely accumulating in proximity. The consciousness framework's insistence on evaluating internal causal organization rather than surface behavior maps directly onto your mechanism-layer principle that the evaluator must assess trajectory, not state: not 'does this look like a coherent concept right now?' but 'is the assembly process still producing real structural gain, or has it plateaued into co-location without integration?' The architecture-over-behavior principle is your stop-condition principle, restated from a different domain.
card.source_shape: The consciousness debate draws a sharp line between behavior and internal causal organization. Any system can be built to say 'I am aware' — that output is cheap to produce and proves nothing. What the serious theories (IIT, global workspace, attention schema) look for instead is whether the system has a unified, integrated, self-modeling causal structure: not what it outputs, but whether the internal machinery is organized in a way that makes those outputs causally coherent from the inside. The diagnostic question is never 'what did it say?' but 'is there a structure here whose trajectory is actually going somewhere meaningful?'
card.use: Consider building the evaluator's completeness probe around structural integration signals rather than output quality signals — specifically, whether newly arriving fragments are forming new load-bearing relationships with existing ones, or whether the marginal structural gain per fragment has flattened to near zero, which is your functional analog to the 'no unified causal organization' finding in consciousness research.
card.limit: The consciousness article is asking a binary, ontological question: is there something it is like to be this system — is a unified subjective experiencer present or not? That is a yes/no question about the nature of a thing. Your evaluator is asking a continuous, functional question: is this process still producing marginal structural gain worth the cost of continuing? There is no analog in your pipeline to the hard problem of consciousness — you do not need a unified experiencer, you need a coherent output artifact, and the theories the article draws on (IIT, global workspace) are specifically designed to detect the presence of a subject, not to measure the progress of an assembly process. The bridge holds for the methodological move — probe internal organization, not surface output — but breaks the moment you try to import any of the specific frameworks or thresholds the consciousness literature uses, because those were built to answer a different question entirely.

known.prior_work_name: Integrated Information Theory (IIT) 4.0
known.reasoning: IIT 4.0 directly documents the bridge's central transfer — evaluating internal causal organization rather than surface behavioral output as the diagnostic criterion for coherence — which the card maps onto the evaluator's need to probe structural integration rather than output quality.

card.spark: Nomad, an autonomous mathematical exploration system, independently invented the same explorer–verifier loop your system needs — a meta-layer that watches trajectory rather than state and pulls resources away from dead ends before they drain the budget.
card.bridge: Your Stage 2 faces the same control problem Nomad solves: you have raw fragments arriving from Stage 1, multiple angles of assembly running in parallel, and a meta-layer that must decide when to keep going, when to redirect, and when to stop — without being told in advance what the finished concept looks like. Nomad's breadth-first topic selection maps directly onto your angle-switching trigger: both are resource-allocation hypotheses about which direction is still yielding. The explorer–verifier separation maps onto your authority timing problem — the evaluator should only assess a partial skeleton once it has enough shape to be assessed, not continuously while fragments are still arriving. And Nomad's sterility detection — weak evidential support flags a branch as exhausted — maps onto your diminishing-returns stop condition: when adding more fragments no longer increases skeleton coherence, that angle is done.
card.source_shape: Nomad traverses a structured mathematical space breadth-first, generating candidate insights at each node. After each candidate forms, a separate verifier checks it against evidence — not to grade the current state, but to decide whether the trajectory that produced it is still fertile. If support is weak, the branch is flagged as sterile and the system reallocates exploration budget to a different angle. The key move is that the verifier never fires until a candidate has actually taken shape, which keeps it from collapsing possibilities too early.
card.use: Consider borrowing Nomad's verifier trigger discipline directly: define a minimum fragment-density threshold that a partial skeleton must reach before the evaluator is allowed to score it — this prevents the meta-layer from killing a branch that simply hasn't had enough material yet, which is exactly the premature closure risk your mechanism layer flags.
card.limit: Nomad's goal is breadth and diversity: it produces many independent, discrete insights across a corpus and never needs them to fuse into one. Your Stage 2 is doing the opposite — converging fragments from multiple branches into a single coherent skeleton. Nomad has no fusion mechanism, no way to detect whether combining two partial structures increases or decreases overall coherence, and no unified artifact being assembled stepwise. Its stop conditions are budget exhaustion and leaf-node depletion, not assembly coherence thresholds. The trajectory-evaluation and sterility-detection logic transfers cleanly; the termination logic and the concept of a growing unified skeleton do not exist in Nomad at all.

known.prior_work_name: Nomad: Autonomous Exploration and Discovery
known.reasoning: The evidence confirms Nomad is a real system for autonomous data exploration that constructs an Exploration Map, generates and verifies hypotheses, and produces diverse reports by discovering new insights — directly performing the bridge's transfer of explorer-verifier loop with sterility-based branch reallocation.

card.spark: A statistical model that infers the hidden rules behind how people draw neighborhood boundaries — deciding which blocks belong and which don't — turns out to share its skeleton with an evaluator that watches fragment-assembly choices and learns which paths are fertile.
card.bridge: Your Stage 2 evaluator faces the same inferential shape. The fragments being assembled are the blocks; the assembly path a wandering agent takes is the boundary-drawing act; and the latent inclusion rule the source model recovers is equivalent to the fertility signal your evaluator is trying to surface. Just as the neighborhood model doesn't need the drawer to articulate their rule — it reads the pattern of what they included — your evaluator could read the pattern of which fragments an agent chose to connect, and update a probabilistic belief about whether that assembly trajectory is converging on a coherent concept or drifting toward sterility. The block-level uncertainty gradient maps directly onto fragment-level inclusion likelihood: instead of a binary 'this assembly is fertile or not,' you get a ranked signal — some fragment combinations are almost always part of coherent concepts, others are contested, and that gradient tells you where to intervene.
card.source_shape: The source model works by observing many people each draw their own subjective neighborhood boundary on a map, then inferring the latent inclusion rule each person was implicitly following — which block-level features (demographics, land use, etc.) made a block feel 'in' or 'out.' It doesn't ask people to explain their rules; it reverse-engineers the rules from the pattern of inclusions and exclusions across many drawing decisions. Crucially, it quantifies uncertainty at each block: some blocks are nearly always included, some are contested, and that gradient of likelihood is itself informative.
card.use: Consider building the evaluator's fertility signal as a learned inclusion-likelihood model rather than a hard coherence classifier — train it on past assembly trajectories to assign each candidate fragment a probability of belonging to the emerging concept, and flag assemblies where too many low-likelihood inclusions accumulate before a concept crystallizes.
card.limit: The source model is a one-time postdiction: it observes a completed boundary drawing and infers the rule after the fact, using fixed demographic covariates that don't change as the drawing proceeds. It has no mechanism for intervening mid-draw, redirecting the drawer, or updating its beliefs in response to new choices as they happen. Your evaluator must do exactly what this model cannot — it needs to read a partially assembled fragment set, form a live belief about fertility, and actively redirect the assembly before the trajectory closes. The bridge holds for the shape of probabilistic inclusion inference; it breaks entirely at the question of dynamic, mid-process intervention.

known.prior_work_name: Measuring and Modeling Neighborhoods (McCartan, Brown, Imai)
known.reasoning: The McCartan et al. paper directly documents the bridge's source shape — a statistical model that infers latent inclusion rules from observed boundary-drawing decisions across many participants, with block-level uncertainty gradients — performing the exact probabilistic inclusion-likelihood inference the card claims.

card.spark: A formal AI system proves it knows when to stop assembling by watching a single number fall until it goes flat — not by checking whether the result looks 'done.' Your evaluator faces the same stopping problem, and the mechanism that solves it there may transfer directly.
card.bridge: Your Layer 2 evaluator must decide when a cluster of collected fragments has cohered into a concept worth surfacing — and the hard problem is knowing when to stop assembling rather than keep pulling in more material. The 4QX disharmony measure maps directly onto that decision: instead of inspecting the assembled concept and judging whether it 'looks complete' (a judgment that has no clean threshold), the evaluator could track a coherence measure across successive assembly steps and watch its trajectory. A measure that is still falling says: keep going, something is still resolving. A measure that has plateaued says: additional fragments are no longer changing the shape of what you have — this is your stop signal. The correspondence holds at every joint: disharmony-falling maps to fragments-still-integrating, plateau maps to diminishing returns, and idempotent equilibrium maps to a concept stable enough to hand off.
card.source_shape: The 4QX system runs a dialectic process over a finite artifact — repeatedly resolving tensions between parts until the whole is internally consistent. It tracks a 'disharmony measure,' a number that is mathematically guaranteed to never increase across steps. When that number stops falling and plateaus, the system has reached idempotent equilibrium: running more cycles changes nothing, so the system stops. Crucially, it never has to ask 'is this complete?' — it only has to ask 'is this still improving?' The plateau is the answer.
card.use: Consider instrumenting the assembly process with an explicit coherence score — something like pairwise semantic tension between fragments, or variance in the concept's inferred boundary — and defining the stop condition as 'this score has not meaningfully moved in N steps' rather than any absolute threshold on the score's value itself.
card.limit: The 4QX result is a proof, not a heuristic: because the artifact is finite and the disharmony measure is formally non-increasing, convergence is guaranteed. Your concept space is open-ended — new fragments can always arrive, and there is no mathematical guarantee that any coherence measure you define will behave monotonically across an unbounded input stream. This means the Lyapunov framing becomes an engineering approximation rather than a convergence certificate: the plateau you detect may be local, temporary, or an artifact of what fragments happened to arrive first. Trust the trajectory signal for triggering a stop, but do not trust it as proof that the resulting concept is globally coherent.

known.prior_work_name: 4QX Holarchy formal proof — Harmony metric H and idempotent equilibrium
known.reasoning: The evidence contains the 4QX Holarchy AI page explicitly documenting the Harmony (H) metric as an engineering measure that decreases monotonically toward zero, with the plateau as the stop condition — directly performing the bridge's transfer of a non-increasing disharmony measure as a trajectory-based assembly stop condition.

card.spark: A deep search agent uses a verifier that asks 'is more retrieval still adding anything?' rather than 'are we done?' — and that distinction is exactly the stop-condition mechanism your meta-layer needs, even though the surrounding architecture is the wrong shape.
card.bridge: Your meta-layer faces the same stop-condition problem the verifier solves: it cannot know when the skeleton is 'complete,' but it can detect when a given angle has stopped producing new structural material. The verifier's marginal-gain test maps directly onto your sterility detection mechanism — an angle is sterile not when it has produced nothing, but when its last N contributions failed to change the skeleton's shape. The merger's role maps onto your fragment-to-concept threshold: integration only happens when incoming material clears a coherence bar, not on a gradient. The trajectory-evaluating logic is sound and portable — it does not depend on the rest of the deep search agent's architecture to function.
card.source_shape: The deep search agent runs a four-step loop: a planner decomposes the user's query into explicit sub-questions, an executor retrieves against those sub-questions, a verifier judges whether the latest retrieval moved understanding forward or just added noise, and a merger integrates what passed. The verifier's key property is that it evaluates trajectory — is marginal gain still positive? — rather than state — have we reached completeness? This matters because completeness has no detectable signal; diminishing marginal gain does.
card.use: Consider lifting the verifier's marginal-gain trigger as a standalone component and wiring it to your allocator: each active angle reports not what it found, but whether its last contribution shifted the skeleton — and the allocator reallocates away from angles whose shift-signal has gone flat.
card.limit: The deep search agent's planner pre-decomposes the query into explicit sub-questions before any retrieval begins, acting as an upstream director that specifies structure in advance. Your architecture requires the opposite: the evaluator/allocator must sit downstream of emergence, observing structure as it forms from fragments rather than prescribing it. Adopting the verifier's logic is safe; adopting the planner's logic would collapse governed emergence into top-down specification, which is the exact failure mode your design is built to avoid. The bridge holds at the stop-condition layer and breaks completely at the control-flow layer.

known.prior_work_name: DuMate-DeepResearch: Auditable Multi-Agent System with Recursive Search and Rubric-Grounded Reasoning
known.reasoning: DuMate-DeepResearch documents a deep search pipeline with explicit plan/execute/validate/merge stages where the verifier evaluates whether retrieval advanced understanding (trajectory check) rather than checking completeness, directly performing the bridge's transfer of marginal-gain verifier logic as a standalone stop-condition component.

card.spark: A practitioner blueprint for multi-agent deep search systems maps almost perfectly onto your Stage 2 architecture by name — but the match is terminological, not structural. Where it breaks is exactly where your hardest problem lives.
card.bridge: Your evaluator/allocator problem — the meta-layer that decides when to switch angles, when to stop, and when a fragment cluster has crossed the threshold into a concept — maps directly onto the planner/validator/improvement-layer stack this workshop names. Sterility detection is the validator's job in their model; reallocation is the planner's job; diminishing-returns triggering belongs to the improvement layer. That decomposition is useful: your 'evaluator' may not need to be a single monolithic meta-layer but a role stack where each function has a distinct structural address. The correspondence holds at the level of 'who does what' — the roles your anchor requires already have named analogs here.
card.source_shape: The workshop decomposes a multi-agent search system into distinct role layers: a planner that routes and decomposes questions, executors that retrieve, a validator that checks consistency, a merger that synthesizes fragments, and an improvement layer that refines the output. Each role is a separate locus of control — the validator does not reallocate, the planner does not synthesize. The system is designed to converge on a clean, structured answer to a known question: it assumes the target exists and treats the pipeline as a reliable path toward it.
card.use: Consider splitting your evaluator/allocator into three structurally separate roles — a trajectory validator that detects sterility, a planner that handles angle reallocation, and an improvement trigger that fires on diminishing returns — rather than designing a single meta-layer that tries to hold all three functions at once.
card.limit: This workshop's architecture assumes the stop condition is arrival: the system halts when it produces a clean, structured answer to a question the user already knew how to ask. Your stop condition is fundamentally different — completeness is undetectable, and the only honest trigger is diminishing returns on a trajectory that was never aimed at a known target. The workshop does not name this asymmetry, let alone solve it; it assumes synthesis is convergent toward a known structure, where your system requires emergence toward an unknown one. The 'continuous improvement layer' is post-hoc polish on a finished answer, not mid-assembly evaluation of whether a concept is coherent enough to exist yet — those are not the same problem.

known.prior_work_name: Beyond Monolithic Architectures: A Multi-Agent Search and Knowledge Optimization Framework for Agentic Search
known.reasoning: This paper directly documents the multi-agent search role decomposition the bridge claims — planner, executor, validator, merger as structurally separate loci of control — performing the transfer of named role-stack decomposition onto the evaluator/allocator design problem.

card.spark: A physical-world knowledge system for industrial workers solved a problem your Stage 2 evaluator hasn't: it split the meta-layer into two structurally distinct roles — a mid-process redirector and a terminal gate — rather than forcing one mechanism to simultaneously steer and stop.
card.bridge: Your Stage 2 evaluator/allocator is trying to do both jobs at once: it must detect when a line of assembly is going sterile and redirect resources mid-process, AND it must decide when the whole skeleton is complete enough to stop. The Flywheel's architecture names exactly why this creates a design tension — the signals that justify redirection are trajectory signals ('this angle is yielding diminishing marginal fragments'), while the signals that justify termination are completeness signals ('the skeleton is coherent enough to close'). These are different questions, fired at different moments, requiring different evidence. Collapsing them into one evaluator forces it to answer a termination question with trajectory data, or delay a reallocation decision until it has completeness evidence it may never get. Splitting them — a mid-process monitor that reallocates without halting, and a terminal gate that enforces stop conditions — lets each mechanism operate on the evidence it can actually read.
card.source_shape: The EON Intelligence Flywheel encodes tacit expert knowledge, deploys it to field workers, and feeds verified observations back into a compounding knowledge base. Its orchestration layer — the Conductor — routes and reallocates resources continuously during the assembly loop, but it never terminates a run. Termination and quality verdicts belong to a separate governance ring that only activates at the exit, after assembly is complete. These two authorities are not just functionally different; they are structurally separated so that mid-loop redirection never collapses into premature shutdown, and terminal gating never bleeds backward into the assembly process.
card.use: Consider whether your current evaluator/allocator is one layer or should become two: a lightweight trajectory monitor that runs continuously and reallocates agent effort when marginal gain drops, feeding into a structurally downstream terminal gate that only fires when the skeleton's internal coherence signals cross a separate threshold — never during assembly.
card.limit: The Flywheel's reallocation and stop triggers are ultimately grounded by physical-world feedback: field reality either confirms or falsifies the assembled knowledge, giving the system an external check that is independent of its own coherence judgments. Your Stage 2 has no such external ground truth — it must assess fertility and completeness purely from internal structural signals like fragment convergence and marginal gain. This makes the Flywheel's clean authority split harder to replicate cleanly: in the Flywheel, 'sterile' and 'done' are eventually anchored to something outside the loop; in your system, both judgments are self-referential, which raises the real risk of premature closure (the evaluator mistakes local convergence for global completeness) or infinite deferral (no internal signal ever crosses a threshold that feels trustworthy enough to stop).

known.prior_work_name: EON Intelligence Flywheel — Conductor orchestration with separate governance ring
known.reasoning: The EON white paper is directly retrievable from the evidence and documents the Conductor mid-process routing layer structurally separated from a terminal governance ring, performing the bridge's transfer of splitting reallocation authority from termination authority into two distinct architectural roles.

card.spark: A middleware scheduling system built for distributed AI nodes accidentally solved a structural problem in your evaluator design: 'redirect resources' and 'halt assembly' are not the same signal, and conflating them into one meta-layer is the source of the friction you've been feeling.
card.bridge: Your current evaluator treats angle-switching and assembly-halting as outputs of the same meta-layer — one process watches the wanderers and decides both 'this angle is exhausted, reallocate' and 'this concept is complete, stop.' The 4QX dual-loop separation says these two judgments operate on fundamentally different signal types. Reallocation is a trajectory signal: it is continuous, comparative, and sensitive to diminishing returns across active angles — it answers 'which direction is still fertile relative to the others?' Termination is a threshold signal: it is discrete, absolute, and sensitive to structural completeness — it answers 'has the skeleton reached sufficient integrity to close?' When one evaluator handles both, trajectory noise bleeds into termination decisions and threshold events trigger premature reallocation. The dual-loop model suggests the fix is not a smarter evaluator but a structural separation: two channels with different input types, different cadences, and a defined coupling between them rather than a shared decision path.
card.source_shape: The 4QX Holon Model runs two causally separate feedback loops. The Activity Loop governs output commitment — it tracks whether a holon's collective ontology and individual actions are staying aligned, and triggers termination when they diverge past a threshold. The Organization Loop governs resource rebalancing — it tracks structural fit between market conditions and self-organizing structure, and triggers split or merge operations when entropy crosses a separate threshold. The key design choice is that these two loops never share a decision path: the signal that says 'stop this holon' is structurally different from the signal that says 'move resources elsewhere,' and the system keeps them separate on purpose.
card.use: Consider splitting your evaluator into two explicitly separate sub-processes — a reallocation channel that runs continuously on trajectory signals (marginal coherence gain per angle, relative fertility across active wanderers) and a termination channel that runs on a discrete structural-completeness check — and then designing the coupling between them as its own explicit interface rather than letting them share state implicitly.
card.limit: The 4QX thresholds — Γ=0.41, H=1.7 nats, void density 10.7% — are presented as universal constants, but they were derived from Taoist numerological priors and post-hoc fitted to middleware scheduling data for GPU slot allocation and P2P node recovery. They have no principled transfer to your problem. Your stop conditions must be endogenous: they need to be derived from the structure of the concept being assembled, specifically from diminishing marginal coherence gain as fragments integrate — not imported from a system that was calibrated to detect when a distributed node cluster has over-partitioned. The structural separation the dual-loop model offers is the transferable insight; the specific numbers are not.

known.prior_work_name: 4QX Holon Model — dual Activity Loop and Organization Loop separation
known.reasoning: The 4QX holon model page documents two causally separate feedback loops — one governing output commitment and one governing resource rebalancing — directly performing the bridge's transfer of structurally separating reallocation signals from termination signals in a distributed AI middleware architecture.

card.spark: Every major theory of consciousness is, at its core, a proposal for detecting a coherence threshold — and that is exactly the problem your evaluator/allocator needs to solve: not 'is the output done?' but 'has integration crossed the point where more assembly stops paying?'
card.bridge: Your evaluator/allocator faces the same structural problem these theories were invented to solve: how does a meta-layer know when the assembly process beneath it has crossed from 'still building' into 'coherent enough to stop'? The fragments your wanderers return are analogous to the distributed neural activations these theories track — individually meaningful, but not yet unified. The coherence threshold in IIT maps directly onto your stop condition: the question is not whether every fragment is present, but whether the marginal integration gain from adding another fragment has dropped below a meaningful threshold. And just as IIT treats Phi as a trajectory indicator — something that rises as integration deepens, not a flag that flips when done — your evaluator could track an analogous integration metric across assembled fragments, watching the curve flatten rather than waiting for a completion signal that will never arrive.
card.source_shape: Competing theories of consciousness — Global Workspace Theory, Integrated Information Theory, Higher Order Thought Theory — each attempt to answer the same structural question: what distinguishes mere information-processing from something qualitatively unified? They answer it with threshold metrics: IIT measures 'Phi,' the degree to which a system's parts are integrated beyond the sum of their independent contributions; GWT tracks whether information has achieved global broadcast saturation across the workspace; Higher Order Thought Theory asks whether the system has formed a meta-representation of its own processing. None of these describe a binary finish line — they describe a measurable degree of integration that, past a certain point, constitutes coherence.
card.use: Consider defining your stop condition as a trajectory signal rather than a state check — instrument the assembly layer to track how much each new fragment changes the coherence of what is already assembled, and treat 'marginal gain falling below threshold' as the evaluator's trigger to close the skeleton and move to synthesis.
card.limit: These consciousness theories are descriptive frameworks — they explain what coherence is, not how to govern an emergence process in real time under resource constraints. Phi is the sharpest example: it is computationally intractable at any meaningful scale, which means it cannot serve as a live operational signal in your pipeline without radical approximation. More fundamentally, every one of these theories assumes a single unified system with no external orchestrator — consciousness, in all of them, is self-arising. Your architecture explicitly requires a meta-layer that sits structurally outside the assembly process and governs it; that separation is exactly what these theories do not accommodate and, in some formulations, actively rule out.

known.prior_work_name: Integrated Information Theory — Phi as trajectory indicator of marginal integration gain
known.reasoning: IIT 4.0 directly documents Phi as a continuous measure of integration that rises as parts become more causally integrated beyond their independent contributions, performing the bridge's transfer of a coherence threshold metric that tracks marginal integration gain rather than a binary completion flag.

card.spark: A clinical AI that decides mid-search whether it has 'enough evidence to stop' is solving the same structural problem as your Stage 2 evaluator — but it encodes its fertility priors into the architecture itself, before any runtime judgment begins.
card.bridge: Your Stage 2 evaluator must answer the same question the oncology agent answers: is what I have assembled so far sufficient to stop, or should I reallocate effort toward a different angle? In the oncology agent, the eleven weighted collections are the architectural answer to 'which perspectives are likely fertile for this query' — the evaluator does not have to reason about that from scratch each time because the prior is baked into the structure. In your system, the analog would be: rather than having your evaluator judge fertility purely from the fragments it receives at runtime, some of that judgment could be encoded upstream — in how your wandering agents are weighted, routed, or filtered before they return material to Stage 2 at all. The evaluator's burden shrinks when the architecture has already pre-eliminated angles that are structurally unlikely to be fertile for the anchor type in question.
card.source_shape: The Precision Oncology agent decomposes a clinical query into a search plan, fans out across eleven weighted vector collections, then runs a sufficiency check mid-execution: do I have enough evidence to synthesize, or do I retry? The eleven collections are not equal — each is weighted by how likely it is to be fertile for a given query type (cancer type, gene, trial status), so the system structurally pre-eliminates sterile angles before semantic ranking even runs. Fertility is not discovered at runtime; it is partially pre-encoded in the collection weights at design time.
card.use: Consider whether your anchor intake step could assign a lightweight 'perspective fertility map' — a structured prior over which agent angles are likely productive for this class of anchor — so your Stage 2 evaluator inherits a pre-filtered hypothesis space rather than treating every incoming fragment as equally worth assembling.
card.limit: The oncology agent's sufficiency check works because clinical queries have known answer shapes — a therapy ranking, a trial match, a gene-drug association — so 'enough structure to stop' is at least partially definable before execution begins, and retry logic operates within a bounded solution space. Your Stage 2 assembles hypotheses from heterogeneous fragments where concept coherence is genuinely emergent: there is no pre-specifiable answer shape, and the stop condition cannot be resolved at design time. This means the oncology agent's architecture can offload fertility judgment to collection weights in a way your system cannot fully replicate — your evaluator will always carry a residual runtime judgment burden that the oncology design sidesteps by operating in a typed domain.

known.prior_work_name: Precision Oncology Intelligence Agent — multi-collection weighted RAG with mid-execution sufficiency check
known.reasoning: The HCLS AI Factory design document directly documents the eleven weighted vector collections with a mid-execution sufficiency check and retry logic, performing the bridge's transfer of pre-encoding fertility priors into collection weights to reduce runtime evaluator burden.

card.spark: A philosophical knowledge architecture designed to organize an entire worldview uses a single coordinating center — called Presence — that holds all domains in coherent relationship without absorbing or overruling any of them. That is exactly the structural problem your evaluator/allocator must solve: govern the wanderers without collapsing their diversity into a single prescribed answer.
card.bridge: Your evaluator/allocator occupies exactly the Presence position. The wandering agents are the domains — each pursuing its own angle freely, generating fragments the evaluator did not and could not prescribe. The evaluator's job is not to direct what any agent finds, but to watch the shape of the emerging whole: which angles are still yielding, which have gone sterile, where the assembly is lopsided. The Harmonism architecture makes explicit what is easy to blur in your design — that meta-control and over-specification are not the same thing, and that a governing layer can be authoritative about structure while remaining genuinely agnostic about content. The failure mode Harmonism guards against — premature closure, where the coordinating center starts filling in domain conclusions rather than holding space for them — is precisely the failure mode your evaluator risks if its sterility signal is too coarse or its reallocation too aggressive.
card.source_shape: Harmonism's Integral Knowledge Architecture arranges domains of knowledge in a fractal 7+1 pattern: seven substantive domains radiate outward, and one coordinating center — Presence — sits not above them but among them, maintaining coherence without issuing directives about content. The center's role is to hold the shape of the whole, notice when a domain is underdeveloped or overextended, and preserve the balance of engagement across all axes. Crucially, Presence never tells a domain what to conclude — it only governs the architecture's structural health, leaving the generative work to the domains themselves.
card.use: Consider auditing your evaluator's decision surface against the Presence/domain distinction: every place the evaluator influences *what* an agent pursues (rather than *whether* to continue or redirect it) is a place where meta-control has drifted into over-specification. Mapping those touch-points explicitly may reveal where the evaluator's authority should stop.
card.limit: Harmonism's architecture is a static classification system applied to a finished corpus — articles are labeled by doctrinal status, breadth, and depth after they exist. It has no mechanism for detecting diminishing returns in real time, no trajectory-based signal that an angle is approaching sterility, and no stop condition tied to marginal gain during an ongoing assembly process. The structural insight — governing without over-specifying — transfers cleanly. The operational question of *when to stop and how to detect it* finds no answer here; for that, you need dynamic models from reinforcement learning or multi-agent task reassignment.

known.prior_work_name: Harmonism Integral Knowledge Architecture — Presence as content-agnostic coordinating center
known.reasoning: The Harmonism methodology page directly documents the 7+1 architecture with Presence as a coordinating center that governs structural health without directing domain content, performing the bridge's transfer of meta-control without over-specification onto the evaluator/allocator design.

card.spark: A methodology built to organize ancient wisdom traditions turns out to share the exact structural skeleton your meta-layer needs — not because your system curates knowledge, but because both face the same hard problem: letting coherent structure emerge from fragments without collapsing it prematurely.
card.bridge: Your meta-layer — the evaluator/allocator sitting above the fusion agents — needs to do exactly what Harmonism's orchestration layer does: govern emergence without over-specifying it. In Harmonism, an architectural pattern is a perspective with a role; in your system, an angle of attack on the anchor plays that role — it's a bounded direction the agents can mine. Harmonism's 'sterility detection' maps directly to your need to recognize when an angle's marginal structural yield has dropped to near zero, triggering reallocation rather than continued waste. And Harmonism's concept coherence threshold — the idea that a fragment doesn't graduate to a concept gradually but crosses a threshold — maps to your fragment-to-concept phase transition: the evaluator isn't watching a gradient climb, it's watching for a discrete snap into coherence.
card.source_shape: Harmonism's integral knowledge architecture provides thirteen patterns for assembling wisdom from multiple traditions into a unified whole. The patterns don't dictate what the final structure looks like — they govern how fragments from different perspectives get evaluated, combined, and promoted into stable concepts. Critically, the system uses a 'knowledge compass' to detect when a given tradition or angle has yielded all it can, and redirects attention to a more fertile perspective rather than forcing more output from an exhausted one.
card.use: Consider modeling your evaluator's angle-switching logic as a small taxonomy of perspective types — each with an expected yield signature and a sterility trigger — so that reallocation is a structured decision about which angle is still fertile, not a reactive abort when something fails.
card.limit: Harmonism was designed for a fundamentally different task: checking whether assembled knowledge is coherent against a pre-existing, bounded ontology. Its evaluator is normative — it asks whether something is right or wrong relative to a known map. Your meta-layer has no such map; it must judge fertility in the absence of a target structure, which is a creative act, not a curatorial one. The pattern vocabulary and compass mechanics are borrowable, but the evaluation criterion cannot be — Harmonism's 'does this fit the tradition' has no analog in a system generating novel structure from nearly nothing.

known.prior_work_name: Harmonism Integral Knowledge Architecture — thirteen patterns with knowledge compass for sterility detection
known.reasoning: The Harmonism methodology page documents the integral knowledge architecture patterns for assembling wisdom from multiple traditions with attention reallocation when a perspective is exhausted, performing the bridge's transfer of governed emergence without over-specification onto a meta-layer evaluator.

card.spark: A journalist's newsroom built on top of a data pipeline solves exactly the problem your meta-layer must solve: raw output accumulates, but nothing is watching whether it's actually converging on a story — until a separate layer with its own memory steps in to judge trajectory.
card.bridge: Your Layer 2 evaluator faces the same shape. The wandering agents in Layer 1 are the pipeline — they produce fragments without any internal sense of whether those fragments are converging. Your meta-layer is the newsroom: structurally downstream of the emergence, never interfering with the collection itself, but holding a running model of what has been assembled so far and asking whether new fragments are advancing it or retreading it. The sterility detection node maps directly onto the newsroom's stall recognition — both are watching trajectory, not state, asking 'are we moving?' rather than 'is this output good?' And the perspective reallocation mechanism maps onto the editorial redirect: when one angle stops producing new signal, authority shifts to a different one, just as an editor reassigns coverage when a beat goes dry.
card.source_shape: The setup is a data pipeline that surfaces market signals week over week, with a newsroom layer sitting above it that holds memory of prior editions and asks whether this week's output connects to the evolving narrative. The newsroom doesn't touch the data collection — it evaluates what the pipeline produced and decides whether it coheres into something worth publishing. The intervention trigger is implicit diminishing returns: if a new edition fails to advance the story, the newsroom layer recognizes the stall and redirects.
