source_cards.spark: A knowledge graph gains credibility not from any single source, but from the density and pattern of back-links accumulating across it — fertility is readable in the topology, not the content. Your evaluator/allocator could do the same: measure structural connectivity, not meaning.
source_cards.bridge: Your evaluator/allocator needs a fertility signal — a way to know whether continuing to send agents down a given angle is still productive or whether that angle has exhausted its marginal contribution. The source gives you a structural answer to that question. A fragment that generates new cross-connections is still fertile: it is touching concept space that hasn't been mapped yet, which is exactly what 'perspective as resource' means in your mechanism layer. A fragment that only reinforces already-established nodes has hit diminishing returns — it is confirming what the skeleton already knows, not extending it. This maps directly onto your 'perspective exhaustion triggers reallocation' node: instead of asking the meta-layer to evaluate the content of what agents are returning, you ask it to evaluate the back-link pattern of what they're returning. Structural signal replaces content judgment, which keeps the meta-layer downstream of emergence rather than upstream of it.
source_cards.source_shape: The source describes a pipeline that converts raw intelligence into concept nodes by tracking how claims link back to each other over time. The key insight is that a concept node earns its place not because someone decided it was important, but because multiple independent fragments keep pointing to it — back-link density is the credibility signal. Two distinct patterns emerge: reinforcement (new fragments confirm existing nodes, adding weight) and emergence (new fragments create cross-connections that didn't exist before, revealing a concept the pipeline hadn't yet named). The system is passive — no active evaluator decides which fragments matter; the link topology does the work.
source_cards.use: Consider operationalizing fertility in your evaluator as a back-link topology check: when an agent's returned fragments are producing new cross-connections in the assembling skeleton, the angle stays open; when they are only reinforcing nodes already present, that angle is declared sterile and resources reallocate. This gives you a stop condition and a reallocation trigger that require no semantic evaluation — only structural pattern detection.
source_cards.limit: The source's pipeline is archival and human-paced: back-links accumulate over weeks as a human reviewer absorbs claims into concept nodes after the fact. Its evaluator is entirely retrospective — it can only read fertility once the links have already formed. Your evaluator/allocator must make prospective resource decisions mid-assembly, before the concept node exists, which means the back-link signal the source relies on simply isn't available at the moment your system needs to act. The source also has no mechanism for angle-switching or session-level stop conditions — all synthesis judgment is deferred to the human, so the part of the pipeline that most resembles your meta-layer is a person, not a function. The topology insight holds as a design direction; the timing and agency assumptions do not transfer.

source_cards.spark: A distributed network of agents with no global view can still detect whether they are converging — not by measuring the answer, but by measuring the shrinking gap between what each agent believes. Your Stage 2 evaluator may not need to judge concept quality at all; it may only need to watch whether the assembly angles are pulling toward each other or drifting apart.
source_cards.bridge: Your Stage 2 evaluator faces the same structural bind the gossip paper dissolves: it cannot directly assess whether an assembling concept is good (the MECHANISM layer flags this as nearly impossible), just as no gossip agent can directly observe the global parameter. The bridge is this — instead of judging the concept, the evaluator watches the *inter-perspective divergence signal*: are the assembly angles producing outputs that are moving toward each other, or are they stable, or are they spreading? Contraction of divergence is the proxy for a healthy trajectory; stagnation or expansion is the signal that reallocation is warranted. This keeps the meta-layer structurally downstream of the emergence it is supposed to oversee — it reacts to trajectory shape, not to content quality it has no legitimate way to assess. The diminishing-returns stop trigger maps directly: when the divergence signal stops contracting, the current allocation has exhausted its fertility, and resources should move.
source_cards.source_shape: The gossip consensus algorithm works by having each agent repeatedly average its local estimate with its neighbors', then apply a stochastic update. No agent ever sees the global state. What guarantees convergence is a Lyapunov function defined not on the estimates themselves but on the *disagreement subspace* — the mathematical residual that measures how far the network is from consensus. When that residual's spectral norm contracts toward zero over time, convergence is happening, even if no individual agent knows it. The stop signal is not 'the answer is correct' but 'the disagreement vector is dying.'
source_cards.use: Consider building the Stage 2 evaluator's reallocation logic around an inter-angle divergence metric rather than any direct quality score — treat contraction of that divergence as evidence of productive convergence, and stagnation or expansion as the signal to reallocate perspective resources to a different angle.
source_cards.limit: The gossip framework treats all disagreement as a defect to be eliminated: every agent is optimizing toward the same objective, so divergence is unambiguously noise to be contracted. In Stage 2, that assumption breaks. Two assembly angles pulling in opposite directions may be the mechanism by which a genuinely emergent concept forms — the divergence is not a failure signal, it is the generative tension the system needs to preserve until a phase transition occurs. Driving inter-angle divergence to zero could be the exact premature closure your ESSENCE layer warns against. The bridge holds for the 'evaluate trajectory, not state' shape and for using divergence dynamics as a reallocation trigger, but it breaks entirely at the question of when divergence should be collapsed versus when it should be actively protected.

source_cards.spark: A branch of mathematics assembles global geometric invariants from scattered local fragments — but only when a 'admissibility' condition is first satisfied, making coherence a gated outcome rather than an automatic one. Your Layer 2 evaluator faces the same gating question: not every collection of fragments deserves to be assembled.
source_cards.bridge: Your Layer 2 faces a structurally similar question at one specific point: before the evaluator promotes a cluster of wanderer-collected fragments into a concept, something must decide whether those fragments are coherent enough to assemble. The admissibility condition maps to that gate — a pre-assembly check that asks whether the fragments are mutually compatible rather than just co-present. Just as the mathematical theory halts construction when admissibility fails, your evaluator might refuse to promote a fragment cluster that hasn't cleared a coherence threshold, preventing noise from being laundered into false concepts. The correspondence holds at the skeleton-assembly moment: fragments in, coherence check, global object out — or not.
source_cards.source_shape: Simplicial Chern-Weil theory constructs characteristic classes — global topological invariants that describe the shape of a geometric object — by stitching together locally-defined connection data collected across overlapping patches of a space. The critical mechanism is an admissibility condition: the local data must satisfy a compatibility constraint before the global assembly is even attempted. If the condition fails, the construction halts; if it holds, the fragments compose into a well-defined global object. The theory never asks 'how much does each patch contribute?' — it asks only 'do these patches fit together at all?'
source_cards.use: Consider making your evaluator's promotion decision explicitly two-phase: a fast coherence gate (do these fragments fit together structurally?) that runs before the slower, costlier question of whether the assembled concept is worth the remaining resource budget. This separates 'can this be assembled' from 'should this be assembled,' which the current architecture may be conflating.
source_cards.limit: The mathematical source is entirely deterministic: it constructs known invariants from known local data, with no autonomous agents, no unknown concepts being discovered, and no notion of trajectory value or diminishing returns. The 'admissibility' gate is binary and static — it fires once and never monitors an ongoing process. Your evaluator must be a dynamic, resource-allocating governor that tracks sterility across time and reallocates budget mid-flight; none of that machinery exists in the source. The bridge holds only at the narrow moment of 'gate before assembling' — it says nothing about how to score, rank, or terminate trajectories.

selection.why_these_cards: Back-link topology gives a fertility signal that is prospective-blind (it only reads after links form). Gossip-disagreement gives a contraction/divergence dynamic readable in real time. Chern-Weil admissibility gives a binary pre-assembly compatibility gate. Each supplies a piece the others lack, and they sit in tension over WHAT a coherence signal even is — accumulated topology vs. dynamic divergence vs. static fit.
selection.spark: Back-link density reads fertility retrospectively; gossip reads convergence dynamically; admissibility reads fit instantaneously. None alone can score a fragment cluster mid-formation. What if the same structural quantity is read at three time-derivatives?
selection.motive: Gives Stage 2 a single fertility instrument that works prospectively, mid-assembly — the timing gap that defeats the back-link card on its own.
selection.tension: Back-link topology insists fertility is only legible AFTER links form (retrospective); gossip insists it's legible from the RATE of inter-perspective movement (prospective); admissibility insists it's a binary gate that fires ONCE. These three cannot all be true of one signal unless the signal is differentiated by order — and that reframing is the discovery.
selection.discovery_path: Card Eb7b4e asserts fertility = back-link density but only readable post-hoc; card S6e752d asserts convergence = contraction of a disagreement residual, readable live; these clash on timing. Resolving the clash: back-link density is a STATE, its first derivative is the rate of new cross-connection formation, its sign-flip (new links vs. mere reinforcement) is what gossip would call contraction. So topology and divergence are the same quantity at two derivatives. Then admissibility (P05-005) supplies the missing zeroth-order check: before measuring rate, confirm the cluster can connect at all. The thesis is a three-derivative connectivity instrument.

blends.thesis: Fertility is a single connectivity quantity read at three time-derivatives: zeroth (admissibility — can these fragments link at all?), first (formation rate — are NEW cross-connections appearing or only reinforcement?), and second (rate-of-rate — is the formation rate itself accelerating, flat, or decaying?). Sterility is not low connectivity; it is a decaying first derivative under a passed admissibility gate.
blends.mechanism: On each fragment arrival: (1) admissibility gate checks structural compatibility with the existing skeleton — fail routes to reallocation with zero further cost; (2) if it passes, classify the resulting links as emergence (new cross-connections) vs. reinforcement (confirming existing nodes); (3) track the emergence-link rate over a sliding window; an angle stays open while that rate is positive and stable, is flagged sterile when it decays toward pure reinforcement. The second derivative distinguishes 'briefly paused' from 'genuinely decaying' — a temporarily flat rate that re-accelerates is not sterile.
blends.emergent_structure: The reframing of a retrospective back-link signal into a prospective one by reading its time-derivatives — neither source card treats connectivity as differentiable-in-time. Back-link card has only the state; gossip card has only abstract residual contraction with no topological content; admissibility card has no time dimension at all. The blend's claim that 'sterility = decaying first derivative of emergence-links under a passed admissibility gate' exists in none of them.
blends.advances_cushion: Gives Stage 2 a concrete, content-blind, mid-assembly fertility instrument — solving the timing gap that the cushion's hardest sub-problem (prospective sterility detection before the concept exists) exposes.

source_cards.spark: A Stoic-adjacent essay on consciousness argues that any system capable of evaluating its own emergence cannot stand outside that emergence — the meta-layer and the process are the same layer. Your evaluator, designed to govern from above, may be subject to the exact deadlock it was built to resolve.
source_cards.bridge: Your Layer 2 evaluator is designed as a meta-layer — something that watches the fragment-assembly process and reallocates effort toward fertile angles, away from sterile ones. The essay's structure maps directly onto the problem you are solving: the wanderers' fragments are the regions of local tension, and the evaluator's job is to detect where tension is crystallizing into concept-coherence versus where it has gone flat. So far the bridge holds. But the essay's deeper claim is the critical one: the allocator cannot stand structurally upstream of emergence and still respond to it accurately — if it is outside the process, it is blind to the gradient signals that make reallocation meaningful. Your evaluator needs those signals to function, which means it must be embedded in the same layer it is trying to govern.
source_cards.source_shape: The essay's model treats a self-organizing system as one where structure unfolds from internal instability rather than external direction. The system moves not because something outside it pushes, but because local regions of high information density pull resources toward themselves — tension accumulates where fragments are crystallizing, and equilibrium (the cancellation of that tension) is the signal that a region is exhausted. Crucially, any layer that evaluates this process must itself be inside it: there is no external vantage point from which a governor can observe without also being subject to the same dynamics.
source_cards.use: Consider whether the evaluator can be redesigned not as a separate controller polling the fragment pool, but as a mechanism that lives inside the relationship network of fragments itself — one that fires reallocation signals when the local density of cross-fragment connections crosses a coherence threshold, and marks sterility when connection attempts stop producing new links rather than when a timer expires.
source_cards.limit: The anchor's entire architecture assumes an intentional meta-layer that can observe the emergence from outside and redirect it — that assumption is what the essay directly attacks. If the essay is right, then an externally-positioned allocator will always be operating on stale or structurally incomplete information, because the signals that distinguish fertile from sterile are only legible from inside the process. This does not mean the evaluator cannot exist; it means the design question of where the evaluator sits is not settled, and building it as a classical controller may reproduce the bootstrap deadlock the system is trying to escape.

source_cards.spark: A physical-world knowledge system for industrial workers solved a problem your Stage 2 evaluator hasn't: it split the meta-layer into two structurally distinct roles — a mid-process redirector and a terminal gate — rather than forcing one mechanism to simultaneously steer and stop.
source_cards.bridge: Your Stage 2 evaluator/allocator is trying to do both jobs at once: it must detect when a line of assembly is going sterile and redirect resources mid-process, AND it must decide when the whole skeleton is complete enough to stop. The Flywheel's architecture names exactly why this creates a design tension — the signals that justify redirection are trajectory signals ('this angle is yielding diminishing marginal fragments'), while the signals that justify termination are completeness signals ('the skeleton is coherent enough to close'). These are different questions, fired at different moments, requiring different evidence. Collapsing them into one evaluator forces it to answer a termination question with trajectory data, or delay a reallocation decision until it has completeness evidence it may never get. Splitting them — a mid-process monitor that reallocates without halting, and a terminal gate that enforces stop conditions — lets each mechanism operate on the evidence it can actually read.
source_cards.source_shape: The EON Intelligence Flywheel encodes tacit expert knowledge, deploys it to field workers, and feeds verified observations back into a compounding knowledge base. Its orchestration layer — the Conductor — routes and reallocates resources continuously during the assembly loop, but it never terminates a run. Termination and quality verdicts belong to a separate governance ring that only activates at the exit, after assembly is complete. These two authorities are not just functionally different; they are structurally separated so that mid-loop redirection never collapses into premature shutdown, and terminal gating never bleeds backward into the assembly process.
source_cards.use: Consider whether your current evaluator/allocator is one layer or should become two: a lightweight trajectory monitor that runs continuously and reallocates agent effort when marginal gain drops, feeding into a structurally downstream terminal gate that only fires when the skeleton's internal coherence signals cross a separate threshold — never during assembly.
source_cards.limit: The Flywheel's reallocation and stop triggers are ultimately grounded by physical-world feedback: field reality either confirms or falsifies the assembled knowledge, giving the system an external check that is independent of its own coherence judgments. Your Stage 2 has no such external ground truth — it must assess fertility and completeness purely from internal structural signals like fragment convergence and marginal gain. This makes the Flywheel's clean authority split harder to replicate cleanly: in the Flywheel, 'sterile' and 'done' are eventually anchored to something outside the loop; in your system, both judgments are self-referential, which raises the real risk of premature closure (the evaluator mistakes local convergence for global completeness) or infinite deferral (no internal signal ever crosses a threshold that feels trustworthy enough to stop).

source_cards.spark: A 3,000-year-old cosmological system encodes a two-register architecture — inviolable raw material in one layer, governed dynamic assembly in the other — that maps directly onto the structural problem of when your meta-layer is allowed to act.
source_cards.bridge: Your Stage 1 output — the raw collected material — is the Xiantian register: it is the pre-manifest scaffold, fixed input that the meta-layer has no authority to touch. Stage 2's assembly process is the Houtian register: the dynamic, in-motion layer where the evaluator/allocator actually operates. The structural insight is that your meta-layer's authority is not a question of how much control to give it — it is a question of which register an operation belongs to. If an operation touches the raw Stage 1 material, the meta-layer has no standing; if an operation belongs to the assembly flux of Stage 2, the meta-layer is exactly where authority should sit. This also resolves the authority timing problem: the evaluator cannot exist before Stage 1 completes, because the Xiantian register must be fully present before the Houtian layer can be governed — the meta-layer is structurally downstream of the scaffold's existence, not a supervisor that was always there waiting.
source_cards.source_shape: The Taoist Bagua divides reality into two registers: Xiantian (pre-manifest, the static structural scaffold that exists before anything happens) and Houtian (manifest, the dynamic layer where actual flux and transformation occur). The meta-governing ratio Γ=0.41 does not float above both registers as a supervisor — it sits structurally downstream of the static scaffold and upstream of the dynamic layer, meaning it can only form after the scaffold exists, and it only ever acts on the manifest flux. The pre-manifest layer is treated as inviolable input: the governing mechanism never reaches back into it to reallocate or revise.
source_cards.use: Consider formalizing the boundary between your two stages not as a handoff but as a register boundary — a hard rule that the evaluator/allocator in Stage 2 is constitutionally prohibited from acting on Stage 1 material, and that this prohibition is what gives it legitimate authority over everything downstream of that line.
source_cards.limit: The Bagua system is closed: 64 hexagram states cover the entire combinatorial space, and the governing mechanism's job is to restore a known equilibrium (Γ=0.41, Fire-Water Harmony). Your pipeline is explicitly open-ended — the concept being assembled is not drawn from a finite library, and coherence is not a return to a pre-specified state but an emergent threshold that cannot be known in advance. This means the termination logics are structurally opposite: the Bagua stops when balance is restored, your system stops when something new has sufficiently crystallized. The two-register structure maps cleanly; the stop condition does not, and any design decision that borrows the homeostatic termination logic from this source will point you in the wrong direction.

selection.why_these_cards: P02-002 argues the evaluator CANNOT stand outside emergence and still read its gradients. S76946a-001 demands the evaluator be SPLIT into a mid-loop redirector and a terminal gate. S79b9d0-002 (Bagua two-register) gives a hard rule about which operations a meta-layer is even allowed to touch. These three collide on the question of WHERE the evaluator sits — inside, split-across, or register-bounded — and the collision forces a non-obvious resolution.
selection.spark: The embeddedness objection (evaluator must be inside) and the authority-split fix (evaluator must be two structurally separate things) seem incompatible — you can't be both embedded AND cleanly separated. Unless the two halves sit in different registers.
selection.motive: Resolves the 'where does the evaluator sit' question that three separate cards each leave unsettled — a load-bearing architecture decision for Stage 2.
selection.tension: P02-002: an external controller is blind, the evaluator must be embedded in the fragment network. S76946a-001: redirection and termination must be STRUCTURALLY SEPARATED or one corrupts the other. These strain against each other — embeddedness wants fusion with the process, the split wants clean separation. S79b9d0 mediates by saying separation is not spatial but by REGISTER.
selection.discovery_path: P02-002 says an outside governor reads stale signals — so the redirector must be embedded in the live fragment flux. S76946a says termination must not bleed into redirection — so the terminal gate must NOT be embedded, or it will fire on trajectory noise. Contradiction: one half embedded, one half not. S79b9d0's two-register model dissolves it: the redirector lives in the Houtian (manifest flux) register, embedded, reading live gradients; the terminal gate lives at the register BOUNDARY, structurally downstream, touching the manifest layer only at exit and never reaching into the flux. The thesis is a register-stratified evaluator.

blends.thesis: The Stage 2 evaluator is register-stratified, not monolithic: a redirector embedded INSIDE the fragment flux (reading live connection-density gradients it could not read from outside) and a terminal gate sitting AT the register boundary (downstream of the flux, blind to live gradients, firing only on boundary-crossing completeness signals). Embeddedness and separation are reconciled because the two functions occupy different registers, not different positions in one register.
blends.mechanism: The redirector is implemented as a property of the fragment relationship network itself — it fires reallocation when local cross-connection density stops producing new links, reading gradients only legible from inside. The terminal gate is a constitutionally separate process that cannot see the flux's instantaneous state; it observes only the cumulative structure crossing the Houtian→exit boundary, and fires when that crossing structure clears a completeness threshold. Because the gate is register-downstream, mid-loop trajectory noise physically cannot reach it; because the redirector is register-embedded, it never goes blind on stale external snapshots.
blends.emergent_structure: The claim that embeddedness and structural-separation are not in conflict because they are properties of DIFFERENT registers — none of the three cards states this. P02-002 has embeddedness but no split; S76946a has the split but treats it as two positions in one space (Conductor vs. governance ring, both observers); S79b9d0 has registers but only one governing ratio, not two stratified evaluator functions. The blend's reconciliation is new.
blends.advances_cushion: Settles the cushion's open architecture question — where the Stage 2 evaluator physically sits — with a structure that simultaneously avoids the blind-controller failure and the conflated-authority failure.

source_cards.spark: A clinical AI that decides mid-search whether it has 'enough evidence to stop' is solving the same structural problem as your Stage 2 evaluator — but it encodes its fertility priors into the architecture itself, before any runtime judgment begins.
source_cards.bridge: Your Stage 2 evaluator must answer the same question the oncology agent answers: is what I have assembled so far sufficient to stop, or should I reallocate effort toward a different angle? In the oncology agent, the eleven weighted collections are the architectural answer to 'which perspectives are likely fertile for this query' — the evaluator does not have to reason about that from scratch each time because the prior is baked into the structure. In your system, the analog would be: rather than having your evaluator judge fertility purely from the fragments it receives at runtime, some of that judgment could be encoded upstream — in how your wandering agents are weighted, routed, or filtered before they return material to Stage 2 at all. The evaluator's burden shrinks when the architecture has already pre-eliminated angles that are structurally unlikely to be fertile for the anchor type in question.
source_cards.source_shape: The Precision Oncology agent decomposes a clinical query into a search plan, fans out across eleven weighted vector collections, then runs a sufficiency check mid-execution: do I have enough evidence to synthesize, or do I retry? The eleven collections are not equal — each is weighted by how likely it is to be fertile for a given query type (cancer type, gene, trial status), so the system structurally pre-eliminates sterile angles before semantic ranking even runs. Fertility is not discovered at runtime; it is partially pre-encoded in the collection weights at design time.
source_cards.use: Consider whether your anchor intake step could assign a lightweight 'perspective fertility map' — a structured prior over which agent angles are likely productive for this class of anchor — so your Stage 2 evaluator inherits a pre-filtered hypothesis space rather than treating every incoming fragment as equally worth assembling.
source_cards.limit: The oncology agent's sufficiency check works because clinical queries have known answer shapes — a therapy ranking, a trial match, a gene-drug association — so 'enough structure to stop' is at least partially definable before execution begins, and retry logic operates within a bounded solution space. Your Stage 2 assembles hypotheses from heterogeneous fragments where concept coherence is genuinely emergent: there is no pre-specifiable answer shape, and the stop condition cannot be resolved at design time. This means the oncology agent's architecture can offload fertility judgment to collection weights in a way your system cannot fully replicate — your evaluator will always carry a residual runtime judgment burden that the oncology design sidesteps by operating in a typed domain.

source_cards.spark: A Family Operating System crystallizes recurring decisions into reusable rules so the governing layer never pays its own design cost twice — your Stage 2 evaluator has the same overhead problem, and the same fix may apply.
source_cards.bridge: Your Stage 2 evaluator faces the same structural tax: if it must re-derive its angle-switching logic and stop conditions from scratch on every run, governance itself becomes a fragmentation source — the very problem it exists to solve. The Family OS's 'build once, deploy often' doctrine maps directly onto this: the evaluator's decision rules (when to switch angles, when diminishing returns have been hit, which fragment clusters are worth pursuing) are candidates for crystallization into reusable heuristics rather than live deliberation. In the parenting system, the meal-plan matrix is not re-invented at dinner time — it is consulted. The structural bridge is that your evaluator could operate the same way: a compiled set of trajectory-reading heuristics that it applies, rather than a reasoning process it re-executes. The 'authority timing problem' in your anchor is partly a re-derivation problem — if the evaluator knew its own rules in advance, it could intervene earlier and cheaper.
source_cards.source_shape: The Parenting Systems Builder constructs a 'Chief of Staff' meta-layer that sits above daily family operations: it centralizes fragmented information (schedules, allergies, milestones) into one Source of Truth, then converts recurring decisions into pre-compiled artifacts — meal-plan matrices, chore rotations, batch-decision protocols. The key mechanism is amortization: the cognitive cost of designing each rule is paid once at build time, then the rule runs cheaply on every future invocation. The meta-layer governs without deliberating fresh each cycle.
source_cards.use: Consider separating your evaluator into two layers: a slow 'design pass' that crystallizes trajectory heuristics from prior runs into explicit, reusable rules, and a fast 'deploy pass' that applies those compiled rules mid-assembly without full re-deliberation — treating governance as a cached artifact that gets updated between runs, not rebuilt inside them.
source_cards.limit: The entire Parenting Systems Builder architecture assumes the domain is closed and enumerable: the inputs (schedules, allergies, calendars) are finite, known in advance, and stable enough to pre-compile rules against. Your Stage 2 operates on genuinely open-ended fragment material where the concept being assembled is not known before assembly begins — there is no pre-existing 'chore rotation' to deploy because coherence looks different for every concept. The 'build once' doctrine breaks exactly where your problem lives: when the output space is undefined, you cannot pre-compile governance rules because you do not yet know what a good trajectory looks like for this particular run. The bridge holds for the overhead-amortization shape, but collapses at the question of what you are actually compiling the rules against.

source_cards.spark: A course-generation pipeline kept producing outputs that passed every schema check yet were completely unteachable — and the fix that worked wasn't better rules, it was a small bank of labeled examples that taught the evaluator what 'moving toward the right register' actually looks like.
source_cards.bridge: Your Layer 2 evaluator faces exactly this shape. The schema-compliance trap maps directly onto the risk of using state-based signals to govern fragment assembly: a fragment can satisfy every structural predicate and still be sterile — pointing nowhere, generating no productive next move. The 40-example calibration set maps onto your 'resource allocation as hypothesis about which angle is fertile' principle: the pipeline discovered that rule-description was an exhausted angle, and exemplar-grounding was the fertile one — not by reasoning about it abstractly, but by measuring which signal actually moved outputs toward the target register. The Bloom's jump is a live instance of diminishing-returns-as-stop-trigger: the rule-description angle had hit its ceiling, and reallocation to a different signal type broke through it. What your evaluator needs is not a better schema — it is a trajectory signal, something that scores whether a fragment is moving toward the target register, not merely whether it currently occupies a valid structural slot.
source_cards.source_shape: The pipeline validated outputs against a structural schema: correct format, correct fields, correct length. Schema compliance was the stop condition. But a content lead's review revealed 23 failures that were structurally valid and pedagogically dead — technically correct, impossible to teach from. Three fixes resolved this: injecting reference material, enforcing length, and replacing abstract taxonomy descriptions with 40 labeled examples as the evaluator's calibration signal. The critical move was that last one — the evaluator stopped asking 'does this match the schema?' and started asking 'does this output look like these known-good examples?' That shift, from state-checking to trajectory-checking, drove Bloom's alignment from 65% to 87%.
source_cards.use: Consider building a small exemplar bank — not a ruleset — as your evaluator's primary scoring instrument: a set of known-good fragments that represent the target register, against which incoming fragments are scored for trajectory alignment rather than schema compliance. This gives your allocator a concrete, adjustable signal for 'this angle is still fertile' versus 'this angle has hit its ceiling.'
source_cards.limit: The structural lesson transfers cleanly, but the mechanism does not — and that gap matters. In the source, every fix was post-hoc: a human reviewed finished outputs on a Friday, logged failures in a spreadsheet, and the team patched the pipeline the following week. There is no mid-process reallocation here, no angle-switching trigger that fires before a fragment hardens, no diminishing-returns halt that redirects resources in real time. The 'evaluator' is a client content lead with a spreadsheet, not a governed-emergence orchestrator. Your Layer 2 must operate mid-assembly — catching sterility before it calcifies into structure — and this source offers no model for how to do that. Take the exemplar-grounding insight; do not take the batch-correction loop as a process template.

selection.why_these_cards: Oncology pre-encodes fertility priors at DESIGN time (collection weights). Family-OS amortizes governance cost by COMPILING rules between runs. Exemplar-bank discovered that rule-descriptions are an exhausted angle and labeled-examples are the fertile signal. They collide on a real conflict: oncology's priors only work in a TYPED domain (the cushion's domain is untyped), Family-OS's compilation only works when the output space is CLOSED (the cushion's is open). Both fixes seem to break on the cushion — until the exemplar card shows what CAN be compiled in an open domain.
selection.spark: Both pre-encoding (oncology) and rule-compilation (Family-OS) are blocked by the cushion's open-endedness — you can't pre-weight angles or pre-compile rules for a concept you don't know yet. But the exemplar card compiled something that survived an open register: not rules, examples.
selection.motive: Lets Stage 2 inherit a pre-filtered hypothesis space and amortized governance WITHOUT pre-specifying the unknown output — attacking the per-run re-derivation tax the cushion implicitly pays.
selection.tension: Oncology and Family-OS both say 'pre-encode the prior, don't reason fresh each run' — but their own limit notes say this only works in TYPED/CLOSED domains, which the cushion is not. The exemplar card directly contradicts the closure requirement: it compiled a fertile signal (40 examples) in an open register where rule-descriptions failed. So compilation IS possible in an open domain — but only of exemplars, not rules or weights.
selection.discovery_path: Oncology says bake fertility into collection weights — blocked, cushion is untyped. Family-OS says compile governance rules between runs — blocked, cushion's output space is open. Both blocks share a cause: you cannot pre-specify structure for an unknown concept. But S8f5a22 shows a team that compiled a TRAJECTORY signal (exemplar bank) precisely because rule-descriptions were sterile in an open register. So the thing that survives compilation in an open domain is not a rule about what coherence IS, but exemplars of what fertile ASSEMBLY-TRAJECTORIES looked like. The thesis: compile trajectory exemplars, not coherence rules.

blends.thesis: In an open-ended assembly domain you cannot pre-compile coherence rules or angle weights (the output is unknown), but you CAN amortize governance by compiling a bank of past fertile-trajectory exemplars — not 'what a good concept looks like' but 'what a still-fertile assembly trajectory looked like at this stage.' The Stage 2 evaluator scores live trajectories by similarity to compiled fertile trajectories, inheriting an amortized prior without ever pre-specifying the concept.
blends.mechanism: Between runs, a slow design pass extracts from prior assemblies the trajectory SHAPES that turned out fertile (rising emergence-link rate, mid-run angle pivots that paid off) and the shapes that turned out sterile (early plateau, pure reinforcement). These become an exemplar bank indexed by assembly STAGE, not by concept content. Mid-run, the fast pass scores the current trajectory's shape against this bank — 'this looks like trajectories that went somewhere' vs. 'this looks like trajectories that died' — giving a pre-trained fertility prior that is content-agnostic and therefore survives the open domain.
blends.emergent_structure: The object being compiled — STAGE-INDEXED TRAJECTORY exemplars rather than concepts, rules, or collection weights. Oncology compiles content-typed collection weights; Family-OS compiles closed-domain rules; the exemplar card compiles known-good OUTPUT fragments (states, not trajectories). None compiles exemplars of trajectory SHAPE indexed by assembly stage — that is the new object, and it is exactly what makes amortization possible in an open domain.
blends.advances_cushion: Removes the per-run governance re-derivation tax the cushion pays, supplying a pre-trained fertility prior that respects the cushion's non-negotiable open-endedness — solving the 'inherit a prior without pre-specifying the answer' bind.

source_cards.spark: The AI consciousness debate draws a hard line between a system that *reports* it is in a state and a system that *actually is* in that state — and your evaluator/allocator is being built entirely on the reporting side of that line.
source_cards.bridge: Your evaluator/allocator is designed to do exactly what access consciousness does: monitor trajectory signals, detect sterility, trigger reallocation. But the debate's structural point lands here with precision — those signals are outputs of a pattern-matching process, not direct reads of whether concept coherence is actually present. This maps onto your mechanism-layer claim that 'completeness is not detectable,' and now gives it a sharper cause: the evaluator can only ever see access-level reports about the assembly lines, never the phenomenal ground truth of whether a concept is genuinely coalescing. The authority timing problem you already have on the board is therefore deeper than it first appears — the evaluator must decide when to trust its stop signals, but those signals are themselves the thing that cannot be fully trusted. A sterile-looking assembly line and a genuinely sterile one are indistinguishable from the evaluator's vantage point if the metrics it uses were trained on proxies rather than coherence itself.
source_cards.source_shape: Philosophers of mind distinguish access consciousness — a system's ability to monitor, report on, and redirect its own internal states — from phenomenal consciousness, the question of whether anything is actually being experienced. The key structural insight is that these two can come fully apart: a system can produce perfectly accurate-sounding reports about its own states while those reports are outputs of pattern-matching against learned signals, not reads of ground truth. 'The system says X' and 'X is actually the case' are logically separable claims, and no amount of behavioral sophistication closes that gap.
source_cards.use: Consider treating the evaluator's stop signals not as readings but as *bets* — and designing the layer to hold multiple competing trajectory assessments in parallel before committing to a reallocation decision, so that premature closure requires a coalition of signals to agree, not just one metric crossing a threshold.
source_cards.limit: The consciousness debate is deliberately unresolved and is designed to stay that way — its honest conclusion is 'we don't know whether access reports track phenomenal reality,' and that epistemic humility is the point of the discourse. Your pipeline cannot afford that posture: it must terminate, it must reallocate, it must produce a working stop condition regardless of whether the stop condition is philosophically certifiable. The analogy accurately names the trap — your evaluator faces irreducible second-order uncertainty about its own signals — but the source offers no exit from the trap, only a precise description of why the trap exists. Engineering the stop condition still falls entirely on you.

source_cards.spark: A distributed network of agents with no global view can still detect whether they are converging — not by measuring the answer, but by measuring the shrinking gap between what each agent believes. Your Stage 2 evaluator may not need to judge concept quality at all; it may only need to watch whether the assembly angles are pulling toward each other or drifting apart.
source_cards.bridge: Your Stage 2 evaluator faces the same structural bind the gossip paper dissolves: it cannot directly assess whether an assembling concept is good (the MECHANISM layer flags this as nearly impossible), just as no gossip agent can directly observe the global parameter. The bridge is this — instead of judging the concept, the evaluator watches the *inter-perspective divergence signal*: are the assembly angles producing outputs that are moving toward each other, or are they stable, or are they spreading? Contraction of divergence is the proxy for a healthy trajectory; stagnation or expansion is the signal that reallocation is warranted. This keeps the meta-layer structurally downstream of the emergence it is supposed to oversee — it reacts to trajectory shape, not to content quality it has no legitimate way to assess. The diminishing-returns stop trigger maps directly: when the divergence signal stops contracting, the current allocation has exhausted its fertility, and resources should move.
source_cards.source_shape: The gossip consensus algorithm works by having each agent repeatedly average its local estimate with its neighbors', then apply a stochastic update. No agent ever sees the global state. What guarantees convergence is a Lyapunov function defined not on the estimates themselves but on the *disagreement subspace* — the mathematical residual that measures how far the network is from consensus. When that residual's spectral norm contracts toward zero over time, convergence is happening, even if no individual agent knows it. The stop signal is not 'the answer is correct' but 'the disagreement vector is dying.'
source_cards.use: Consider building the Stage 2 evaluator's reallocation logic around an inter-angle divergence metric rather than any direct quality score — treat contraction of that divergence as evidence of productive convergence, and stagnation or expansion as the signal to reallocate perspective resources to a different angle.
source_cards.limit: The gossip framework treats all disagreement as a defect to be eliminated: every agent is optimizing toward the same objective, so divergence is unambiguously noise to be contracted. In Stage 2, that assumption breaks. Two assembly angles pulling in opposite directions may be the mechanism by which a genuinely emergent concept forms — the divergence is not a failure signal, it is the generative tension the system needs to preserve until a phase transition occurs. Driving inter-angle divergence to zero could be the exact premature closure your ESSENCE layer warns against. The bridge holds for the 'evaluate trajectory, not state' shape and for using divergence dynamics as a reallocation trigger, but it breaks entirely at the question of when divergence should be collapsed versus when it should be actively protected.

source_cards.spark: Nomad, an autonomous mathematical exploration system, independently invented the same explorer–verifier loop your system needs — a meta-layer that watches trajectory rather than state and pulls resources away from dead ends before they drain the budget.
source_cards.bridge: Your Stage 2 faces the same control problem Nomad solves: you have raw fragments arriving from Stage 1, multiple angles of assembly running in parallel, and a meta-layer that must decide when to keep going, when to redirect, and when to stop — without being told in advance what the finished concept looks like. Nomad's breadth-first topic selection maps directly onto your angle-switching trigger: both are resource-allocation hypotheses about which direction is still yielding. The explorer–verifier separation maps onto your authority timing problem — the evaluator should only assess a partial skeleton once it has enough shape to be assessed, not continuously while fragments are still arriving. And Nomad's sterility detection — weak evidential support flags a branch as exhausted — maps onto your diminishing-returns stop condition: when adding more fragments no longer increases skeleton coherence, that angle is done.
source_cards.source_shape: Nomad traverses a structured mathematical space breadth-first, generating candidate insights at each node. After each candidate forms, a separate verifier checks it against evidence — not to grade the current state, but to decide whether the trajectory that produced it is still fertile. If support is weak, the branch is flagged as sterile and the system reallocates exploration budget to a different angle. The key move is that the verifier never fires until a candidate has actually taken shape, which keeps it from collapsing possibilities too early.
source_cards.use: Consider borrowing Nomad's verifier trigger discipline directly: define a minimum fragment-density threshold that a partial skeleton must reach before the evaluator is allowed to score it — this prevents the meta-layer from killing a branch that simply hasn't had enough material yet, which is exactly the premature closure risk your mechanism layer flags.
source_cards.limit: Nomad's goal is breadth and diversity: it produces many independent, discrete insights across a corpus and never needs them to fuse into one. Your Stage 2 is doing the opposite — converging fragments from multiple branches into a single coherent skeleton. Nomad has no fusion mechanism, no way to detect whether combining two partial structures increases or decreases overall coherence, and no unified artifact being assembled stepwise. Its stop conditions are budget exhaustion and leaf-node depletion, not assembly coherence thresholds. The trajectory-evaluation and sterility-detection logic transfers cleanly; the termination logic and the concept of a growing unified skeleton do not exist in Nomad at all.

selection.why_these_cards: Access-vs-phenomenal says the evaluator's signals are bets, never reads of ground truth — so commit only on a COALITION of signals. Gossip says drive divergence to zero to detect convergence — but its own limit warns divergence may be the GENERATIVE tension to PROTECT, not collapse. Nomad says never let the verifier fire until a candidate has SHAPE. Three cards, three different reasons the evaluator might pull the trigger wrong: untrustworthy signals, mistaking generative divergence for noise, firing too early. Their collision builds a single discipline against premature closure.
selection.spark: Gossip wants divergence → 0 as the convergence signal, but its own footnote says that exact move could be the premature closure the system most fears. So divergence collapse is ambiguous: it could mean 'converging' or 'killing the generative tension.' How do you tell which? Coalition of signals (access card) under a shape-gate (Nomad).
selection.motive: Builds the cushion's central safeguard — a stop/collapse trigger that resists premature closure when the very signal of progress (divergence collapse) is also the signature of failure.
selection.tension: Gossip: contracting divergence = healthy convergence, collapse it. Its OWN limit + access card: collapsing divergence may destroy the generative tension AND the signal is only a bet anyway. Nomad adds: don't even evaluate until shape exists. So one card says 'collapse divergence to win' and the others say 'collapsing divergence may be exactly how you lose' — an irreducible ambiguity in the single most natural stop signal.
selection.discovery_path: Gossip offers divergence-contraction as the convergence proxy. Access card warns no single signal is a ground-truth read — require a coalition. But coalition of WHAT, when divergence-collapse is itself ambiguous between 'converged' and 'tension killed'? Resolve by adding a second, orthogonal signal that disambiguates: Nomad's shape-gate says a candidate must have taken structural form before judgment. So: divergence collapse only counts as convergence IF a coherent shape has ALSO formed; divergence collapse WITHOUT shape means the generative tension was prematurely killed. The coalition that disambiguates is {divergence-contraction, shape-present}, and both must agree before closure.

blends.thesis: Divergence collapse is a two-faced signal — it means 'concept converged' only when paired with structural shape, and means 'generative tension prematurely killed' when shape is absent. The evaluator must therefore require a disambiguating COALITION: it may close the skeleton only when divergence is contracting AND a load-bearing shape has formed; contracting divergence with no shape is the premature-closure alarm, triggering protection of the tension rather than commitment.
blends.mechanism: Run two orthogonal monitors. Monitor A tracks inter-angle divergence (gossip residual). Monitor B tracks whether a structurally integrated shape has emerged (Nomad's shape-gate / load-bearing link presence). Truth table: divergence-contracting + shape-present → close (genuine convergence); divergence-contracting + shape-absent → ALARM, the angles agreed before structure formed, protect/re-inject divergence rather than close; divergence-stable + shape-present → reallocate, fertile but not yet converged; divergence-expanding + shape-absent → early exploration, hold. No single monitor can fire closure alone, satisfying the access-card requirement that commitment needs a coalition because each signal is only a bet.
blends.emergent_structure: The truth table that makes divergence-collapse CONDITIONAL on shape — neither source has it. Gossip treats divergence contraction as unconditionally good; the access card argues for coalitions abstractly but names no specific orthogonal pair; Nomad has the shape-gate but never connects it to divergence dynamics. The blend's specific claim — 'contracting divergence WITHOUT shape is the premature-closure alarm' — is the new structure that turns an ambiguous signal into a decidable one.
blends.advances_cushion: Directly arms the cushion's most-named fear (premature closure) with an operational rule that distinguishes genuine convergence from tension-collapse, using only trajectory signals the evaluator can actually read.

references.title: A Conversational Approach to Process-oriented Case-based Reasoning
Advanced Search Techniques for Determining Optimal Sequences of Adaptation Rules in Process-Oriented Case-Based Reasoning | Springer Nature Link

adjacent.resemblance: Process-Oriented Case-Based Reasoning (POCBR) explicitly indexes past cases by process/workflow shape rather than output content, retrieves them by process similarity, and adapts them to new open-ended tasks — this is a near-twin of the core move. Amortized Bayesian Workflow compiles a prior from past inference trajectories to score new ones without re-running full inference.
adjacent.still_new: POCBR indexes workflow cases but does not specifically index by ASSEMBLY STAGE within a trajectory (i.e., 'what did fertile trajectories look like at stage N of assembly'), nor does it apply this to an open-ended concept-formation domain where the output is unknown and content-agnostic fertility is the scoring criterion. The specific combination of stage-indexing + fertility-shape (not workflow-shape) + content-agnostic open-domain application is not performed by any hit.
adjacent.reasoning: POCBR performs a genuinely close cousin of this move — indexing past process trajectories for retrieval and adaptation in open-ended tasks — making this ADJACENT rather than NOVEL. However, no hit combines stage-indexed trajectory shapes with a fertility signal (emergence-link rate curves) as the indexing key in a content-agnostic open-domain assembly system; that specific transfer remains unprecedented.

references.title: [1911.11395] Creativity in temporal social networks: How divergent thinking is impacted by one's choice of peers
Filtration-Based Representation Learning for Temporal Graphs

novel.resemblance: Network dynamics of innovation processes tracks new-edge formation rates in evolving knowledge graphs; temporal social network creativity work links connectivity patterns to generative output; filtration-based temporal graph work tracks topological changes over time. These share the ingredients: dynamic graphs, edge-rate tracking, creativity/fertility correlation.
novel.reasoning: No hit performs the specific move of reading connectivity as a differentiable-in-time quantity at three simultaneous derivative orders (zeroth=admissibility gate, first=emergence-link rate, second=rate-of-rate distinguishing 'paused' from 'decaying'), nor does any hit define sterility as 'decaying first derivative of emergence-links under a passed admissibility gate' as a prospective signal. The closest work tracks aggregate new-edge rates or topological filtrations but never stratifies the signal into three derivative layers with the admissibility gate as a precondition and the second derivative as a pause-vs-decay disambiguator. The move — treating the back-link signal as differentiable-in-time with this specific three-layer architecture for prospective fertility forecasting — is not performed by any hit.

references.title: [2011.04576] Distributed Design of Glocal Controllers via Hierarchical Model Decomposition
[2602.13830] A Tale of Two Graphs: Separating Knowledge Exploration from Outline Structure for Open-Ended Deep Research
[2002.05954] Learning Functionally Decomposed Hierarchies for Continuous Control Tasks with Path Planning

novel.resemblance: Hierarchical/glocal controller work separates local embedded sensors from global supervisory controllers; 'Tale of Two Graphs' separates exploration graph from outline structure graph; functionally decomposed hierarchies split local reactive control from global path planning. These share the ingredient of two functionally separated evaluators at different abstraction levels.
novel.reasoning: No hit performs the specific move of reconciling embeddedness and structural-separation by assigning them to DIFFERENT REGISTERS rather than different positions in one register — and deriving from that register-difference the physical impossibility of mid-loop noise reaching the terminal gate. The hierarchical control literature separates local from global but treats both as observers in a shared state space; 'Tale of Two Graphs' separates exploration from structure but both graphs are co-present and mutually visible. The blend's claim that the apparent contradiction between embeddedness and separation dissolves because the two functions occupy different registers (not different positions in one register) is not a move any hit performs.

references.title: The Hidden Strength of Disagreement: Unraveling the Consensus-Diversity Tradeoff in Adaptive Multi-Agent Systems
Premature Containment in Human-AI Interaction — Signal Literature

novel.resemblance: Consensus-diversity tradeoff work recognizes that premature consensus is harmful and that diversity should be preserved; premature containment work identifies sequencing failures where closure fires before structure is ready; semantic consensus work tracks conflict detection before resolution. These share the ingredient of treating convergence signals as potentially premature.
novel.reasoning: No hit performs the specific move of constructing a two-monitor truth table where divergence-contraction is made CONDITIONAL on an orthogonal structural-shape signal, such that contracting divergence with absent shape triggers a protection/re-injection alarm rather than closure. The consensus literature treats divergence contraction as either good or bad globally; no hit pairs a gossip-residual-style divergence monitor with a load-bearing-shape monitor and derives a four-cell truth table that makes each cell decidable and assigns 'contracting divergence + no shape = premature-closure alarm' as a specific actionable state. That specific conditional disambiguation move is not performed by any hit.

# Semantic Consensus F…

trace.discovery_path: Card Eb7b4e asserts fertility = back-link density but only readable post-hoc; card S6e752d asserts convergence = contraction of a disagreement residual, readable live; these clash on timing. Resolving the clash: back-link density is a STATE, its first derivative is the rate of new cross-connection formation, its sign-flip (new links vs. mere reinforcement) is what gossip would call contraction. So topology and divergence are the same quantity at two derivatives. Then admissibility (P05-005) supplies the missing zeroth-order check: before measuring rate, confirm the cluster can connect at all. The thesis is a three-derivative connectivity instrument.
trace.thesis: Fertility is a single connectivity quantity read at three time-derivatives: zeroth (admissibility — can these fragments link at all?), first (formation rate — are NEW cross-connections appearing or only reinforcement?), and second (rate-of-rate — is the formation rate itself accelerating, flat, or decaying?). Sterility is not low connectivity; it is a decaying first derivative under a passed admissibility gate.

trace.discovery_path: P02-002 says an outside governor reads stale signals — so the redirector must be embedded in the live fragment flux. S76946a says termination must not bleed into redirection — so the terminal gate must NOT be embedded, or it will fire on trajectory noise. Contradiction: one half embedded, one half not. S79b9d0's two-register model dissolves it: the redirector lives in the Houtian (manifest flux) register, embedded, reading live gradients; the terminal gate lives at the register BOUNDARY, structurally downstream, touching the manifest layer only at exit and never reaching into the flux. The thesis is a register-stratified evaluator.
trace.thesis: The Stage 2 evaluator is register-stratified, not monolithic: a redirector embedded INSIDE the fragment flux (reading live connection-density gradients it could not read from outside) and a terminal gate sitting AT the register boundary (downstream of the flux, blind to live gradients, firing only on boundary-crossing completeness signals). Embeddedness and separation are reconciled because the two functions occupy different registers, not different positions in one register.

trace.discovery_path: Oncology says bake fertility into collection weights — blocked, cushion is untyped. Family-OS says compile governance rules between runs — blocked, cushion's output space is open. Both blocks share a cause: you cannot pre-specify structure for an unknown concept. But S8f5a22 shows a team that compiled a TRAJECTORY signal (exemplar bank) precisely because rule-descriptions were sterile in an open register. So the thing that survives compilation in an open domain is not a rule about what coherence IS, but exemplars of what fertile ASSEMBLY-TRAJECTORIES looked like. The thesis: compile trajectory exemplars, not coherence rules.
trace.thesis: In an open-ended assembly domain you cannot pre-compile coherence rules or angle weights (the output is unknown), but you CAN amortize governance by compiling a bank of past fertile-trajectory exemplars — not 'what a good concept looks like' but 'what a still-fertile assembly trajectory looked like at this stage.' The Stage 2 evaluator scores live trajectories by similarity to compiled fertile trajectories, inheriting an amortized prior without ever pre-specifying the concept.

trace.discovery_path: Gossip offers divergence-contraction as the convergence proxy. Access card warns no single signal is a ground-truth read — require a coalition. But coalition of WHAT, when divergence-collapse is itself ambiguous between 'converged' and 'tension killed'? Resolve by adding a second, orthogonal signal that disambiguates: Nomad's shape-gate says a candidate must have taken structural form before judgment. So: divergence collapse only counts as convergence IF a coherent shape has ALSO formed; divergence collapse WITHOUT shape means the generative tension was prematurely killed. The coalition that disambiguates is {divergence-contraction, shape-present}, and both must agree before closure.
trace.thesis: Divergence collapse is a two-faced signal — it means 'concept converged' only when paired with structural shape, and means 'generative tension prematurely killed' when shape is absent. The evaluator must therefore require a disambiguating COALITION: it may close the skeleton only when divergence is contracting AND a load-bearing shape has formed; contracting divergence with no shape is the premature-closure alarm, triggering protection of the tension rather than commitment.
